IN THE CONGRESS OF THE UNITED STATES

A BILL

To restore the constitutional balance of power between the Federal Government and the people of the United States by addressing the consolidation of executive authority, the erosion of congressional oversight, the asymmetry of surveillance, intelligence, and coercive capabilities between the government and the governed, and the structural conditions that have rendered the people’s capacity to hold their government accountable unequal to the government’s capacity to monitor, coerce, and control them; and for other purposes.

Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled,

SEC. 1. SHORT TITLE; TABLE OF CONTENTS.

(a) Short Title.—This Act may be cited as the “Mass Accountability in Democracy Act” or the “MAD Act.”

(b) Designation.—The designation of this Act reflects the scope and ambition of the legislative response it contains. The word “Mass” refers to the scale of the accountability this Act demands—accountability that is not selective, not incremental, and not limited to a single institution, agency, or domain of governmental power, but comprehensive in scope and binding in application across a range of structural imbalances between the Federal Government and the people it serves. Democratic self-governance, through a constitutional federal republic, cannot be maintained when accountability is optional, episodic, or dependent on the willingness of the powerful to submit to scrutiny. This Act is designed to make accountability structural, systemic, and unavoidable in the domains it covers.

(c) Table of Contents.—The table of contents of this Act is as follows:

Sec. 1. Short title; table of contents.

Sec. 2. Findings and purpose.

TITLE I—SURVEILLANCE PROHIBITION, PRIVACY, AND EXECUTIVE ACCOUNTABILITY

Subtitle A—American Surveillance Prohibition and Privacy Enforcement Act (Secs. 1001–1166)

Subtitle B—Executive Accountability and Judicial Oversight Act (Secs. 1201–1263)

TITLE II—DEMANDING A PLAN FOR AI (Secs. 2001–2023)

TITLE III—INSURRECTION ACT REFORM (Secs. 3001–3006)

TITLE IV—POLITICAL SPENDING DISCLOSURE (Secs. 4001–4022)

TITLE V—MASS DIGITIZATION AND PRESERVATION OF FEDERAL GOVERNMENT RECORDS AND COLLECTIONS (Secs. 5001–5013)

TITLE VI—VOTER CHOICE AND ELECTION MODERNIZATION (Secs. 6001–6010)

Subtitle A—Definitions and Conditional Amendments for Alternative Voting Methods in Federal Elections

Subtitle B—Grants for State and Local Alternative Voting Method Adoption

Subtitle C—General Provisions

TITLE VIII—FEDERAL IMMIGRATION ENFORCEMENT AGENT SAFETY AND ACCOUNTABILITY TRAINING (Secs. 8001–8012)

TITLE IX—ACCOUNTABILITY AND TRANSPARENCY IN POLITICAL VIOLENCE INVESTIGATIONS (Secs. 9001–9010)

SEC. 2. FINDINGS AND PURPOSE.

(a) Findings.—Congress finds the following:

(1) The Constitution of the United States was designed to prevent the accumulation of unchecked power in any single institution. James Madison warned in Federalist No. 47 that “[t]he accumulation of all powers, legislative, executive, and judiciary, in the same hands, whether of one, a few, or many, and whether hereditary, self-appointed, or elective, may justly be pronounced the very definition of tyranny.” The structural architecture of the Constitution—separation of powers, checks and balances, enumerated authorities, and reserved rights—reflects the Framers’ considered judgment that no government, however well-intentioned at its founding, can be trusted to restrain itself.

(2) Madison further warned, at the Constitutional Convention on June 29, 1787, that “[a] standing military force, with an overgrown Executive will not long be safe companions to liberty. The means of defence agst. foreign danger, have been always the instruments of tyranny at home.” Congress finds that this warning has acquired a gravity that Madison could not have foreseen: the instruments of defense against foreign danger now include mass surveillance systems, artificial intelligence, autonomous weapons platforms, and intelligence collection capabilities of a scope and power without precedent in human history—and that these instruments are increasingly turned inward against the people they were built to protect.

(3) Thomas Jefferson observed that “[e]xperience hath shewn, that even under the best forms of government those entrusted with power have, in time, and by slow operations, perverted it into tyranny.” Madison, in a speech to the Virginia Convention in 1788, cautioned that “there are more instances of the abridgement of freedom of the people by gradual and silent encroachments by those in power than by violent and sudden usurpations.” Congress finds that the structural imbalances addressed by this Act are the product of precisely such gradual encroachments—no single statute, executive order, or technological development created them, but their cumulative effect has produced a condition in which the executive branch possesses coercive, surveillance, and information-processing capabilities that dwarf any countervailing power available to the people or to the Congress.

(4) Madison wrote in Federalist No. 51 that “[a] dependence on the people is, no doubt, the primary control on the government; but experience has taught mankind the necessity of auxiliary precautions.” Congress finds that both the primary control and the auxiliary precautions have been degraded. The primary control—popular accountability through elections—has been undermined by dark money, foreign financial influence, and information manipulation. The auxiliary precautions—congressional oversight, judicial review, freedom of the press, and transparency requirements—have been systematically eroded by executive assertions of privilege, the classification system, the weaponization of national security claims, and the sheer scale of executive operations that exceed the institutional capacity of Congress to monitor.

(5) Madison observed in a letter to W.T. Barry on August 4, 1822, that “[a] popular Government without popular information, or the means of acquiring it, is but a Prologue to a Farce or a Tragedy, or perhaps both. Knowledge will forever govern ignorance: And a people who mean to be their own Governors, must arm themselves with the power which knowledge gives.” Congress finds that the information asymmetry between the Federal Government and the American people has reached a scale incompatible with democratic self-governance. The government maintains the capacity to collect, aggregate, and computationally analyze information about virtually every aspect of its citizens’ lives, while the vast majority of the government’s own records—documenting its decisions, operations, and conduct across the full history of the Republic—remain in analog form, inaccessible to the public and to computational analysis.

(6) Congress finds that the executive branch of the United States Government has, over successive decades, accumulated capabilities in surveillance, intelligence collection, data analysis, military force, covert operations, and information control that were neither contemplated by the Framers nor authorized by any single act of Congress—and that the aggregate effect of these capabilities is a structural power asymmetry between the government and the governed that is fundamentally incompatible with the constitutional design. This asymmetry is manifest across multiple dimensions of governmental power, including but not limited to the following:

(A) Commercial data brokers, platform operators, and intelligence contractors have assembled databases covering hundreds of millions of American citizens. Federal agencies have systematically purchased access to these compilations to acquire information they could not constitutionally compel through warrant, subpoena, or other lawful process—a circumvention strategy the Supreme Court’s holding in Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. 296 (2018), establishes is constitutionally impermissible. The government’s capacity to monitor the daily lives of its citizens now vastly exceeds the citizens’ capacity to monitor the operations of their government.

(B) The executive branch commands standing military forces, federalized law enforcement agencies, and intelligence services whose combined capabilities bear no proportion to any countervailing force available to the civilian population. The Insurrection Act, as currently codified, provides virtually unchecked presidential authority to deploy those forces domestically, with no meaningful time limit, no mandatory congressional consultation, no judicial review, and triggering language so vague that it could be invoked against peaceful protest or political dissent.

(C) The classification system, executive privilege claims, deliberative process privilege, and the operational complexity of modern government render vast categories of executive action invisible to the public and to Congress. Meanwhile, the government’s own institutional records—documenting decisions, operations, and conduct across more than two centuries—remain overwhelmingly in analog form, inaccessible to the computational tools that the government itself uses to analyze information about its citizens.

(D) Artificial intelligence systems are advancing at an exponential rate and are being deployed across employment, healthcare, finance, national security, education, law enforcement, and critical infrastructure with transformative but insufficiently understood consequences. No comprehensive federal regulatory framework governs their development or deployment. The government possesses the institutional capacity to develop and deploy AI systems; the public lacks any corresponding capacity to audit, oversee, or constrain those deployments.

(E) Dark money spending in Federal elections has grown from less than $5,000,000 in 2006 to approximately $1,900,000,000 in the 2024 election cycle. The same undisclosed financial networks that shape electoral outcomes also shape the composition of the Federal judiciary, acquiring disproportionate influence across multiple branches of government that operates beyond the reach of public accountability.

(F) When the executive branch is directed by statute to disclose information of compelling public interest, structural incentives render compliance systematically unreliable absent independent oversight. Officials who over-redact, miss statutory deadlines, or assert unauthorized privileges face no personal or institutional consequence. The documented violations of the Epstein Files Transparency Act, Public Law 119–38—enacted with a vote of 427 to 1 in the House and unanimous consent in the Senate—illustrate this structural failure.

(7) Congress finds that its own institutional capacity to serve as an effective check on executive power has been degraded by a combination of structural, political, and informational factors. The legislative branch lacks the technical expertise, staffing, and institutional infrastructure to conduct effective oversight of executive branch activities in domains including artificial intelligence, surveillance technology, and cybersecurity. Congressional oversight committees are routinely denied access to information on the grounds of classification, executive privilege, or ongoing investigation. The pace of technological change and executive action has outstripped the legislative process.

(8) Public trust in Congress is at historic lows. The combination of partisan dysfunction, the influence of undisclosed political spending, and the demonstrable failure of Congress to enact legislation addressing known and documented harms—including harms to children from AI systems, the unregulated growth of mass surveillance, the absence of meaningful data privacy protections, and the militarization of domestic law enforcement—has created a crisis of democratic legitimacy that this Act seeks to address.

(9) The power of the people to hold their government accountable depends upon the availability of information, the transparency of political financing, the integrity of the electoral process, the restraint of coercive force, and the existence of meaningful legal remedies when the government exceeds its authority. Each of these conditions has been degraded. This Act addresses them comprehensively because the degradation is comprehensive, and because the structural imbalance between government and governed cannot be remedied by addressing any single dimension in isolation.

(10) Commercial data brokers have assembled databases of unprecedented scale covering hundreds of millions of Americans without meaningful knowledge or consent. These databases enable inference-based psychological manipulation, operational targeting of individuals based on their locations and routines, and totalizing population-scale surveillance. The documented history of the FBI’s COINTELPRO program—exposed in 1971 and investigated by the Church Committee in 1975 and 1976—demonstrates that domestic intelligence agencies will, absent categorical legal constraints, use surveillance capabilities to target and suppress constitutionally protected political activity. The Cambridge Analytica operation demonstrated that commercial psychological profiling infrastructure constitutes a direct threat to democratic self-governance. No comprehensive federal privacy statute adequate to address these threats exists.

(11) Artificial intelligence systems are being deployed at scale to, and accessed by, minors without adequate safety standards, clinical validation, or legal oversight, contributing to documented deaths. The International Monetary Fund estimates that approximately 40 percent of all jobs globally are exposed to AI-driven change, with 60 percent exposure in advanced economies. AI-generated child sexual abuse material reports increased by 1,325 percent in a single year. AI-generated synthetic media depicting political candidates has proliferated during Federal election cycles without comprehensive federal regulation. The Federal Government lacks the institutional capacity, comprehensive evidentiary record, and standardized measurement methodologies necessary to prescribe effective, durable, and judicially sustainable AI regulatory standards.

(12) Since the Supreme Court’s 2010 decision in Citizens United v. FEC, dark money groups have spent at least $4,300,000,000 on Federal elections. Total outside spending on 2024 Federal elections reached a record $4,500,000,000, with more than half coming from groups that do not fully disclose the source of their funding. Shell companies and nonprofits contributed $1,300,000,000 to super PACs during the 2024 election cycle alone—more than in the prior two cycles combined. Well-funded networks of organizations that do not disclose their donors have simultaneously spent hundreds of millions of dollars to influence the nomination and confirmation of Federal judges, creating conditions in which the same anonymous networks shape who holds elected office, who sits on the Federal bench, and what cases and arguments reach those judges. The opacity of these channels creates pathways through which foreign money may enter domestic elections undetected. Transparency in the financing of elections and judicial nominations is a necessary condition for the informed self-governance that each title of this Act seeks to protect.

(13) The Insurrection Act, as currently codified at 10 U.S.C. §§ 251–255, contains triggering language so vague that it could authorize the deployment of combat forces in response to two individuals conspiring to commit a minor federal offense. The Act authorizes the President to respond “by using the militia or the armed forces, or both, or by any other means,” a delegation so broad that it could be construed to authorize the deputization of private paramilitary organizations or private military contractors as instruments of federal authority. The Act contains no statutory time limit on deployment, no requirement of congressional consultation or reporting, and no meaningful judicial review. On multiple recent occasions, the Act has been threatened or considered for use against peaceful protest, political dissent, immigration enforcement operations, and the processes of electoral certification. The Act’s broadest provisions were written for conditions that no longer exist—the absence of professional law enforcement, the infancy of federal agencies, and the aftermath of the Civil War.

(14) In August 2025, the Department of Homeland Security cut approximately 240 hours—more than 40 percent—from ICE basic training, eliminating all legal instruction on use of force, reducing firearms instruction by half, and cutting more than 100 hours of hands-on instruction. Simultaneously, ICE expanded from approximately 10,000 to 22,000 officers—an increase of 120 percent—while reducing the training each new officer received. The resulting deployment of undertrained agents into urban civilian environments created foreseeable conditions that contributed to the deaths of American citizens, including the killing of Alex Jeffrey Pretti, age 37, a United States military veteran and intensive care nurse, who was shot ten times in under five seconds by federal agents while directing traffic and assisting a fallen bystander in Minneapolis, Minnesota. European democracies that require two to three years of law enforcement training have police killing rates between 25 and 80 times lower than the United States on a per capita basis. Randomized controlled trials have demonstrated that structured de-escalation training produces significant and measurable reductions in use-of-force incidents, civilian injuries, and officer injuries.

(15) The National Archives and Records Administration holds an estimated 13,500,000,000 pages of textual records, of which fewer than 3.4 percent have been digitized and made available to the public. The Library of Congress holds more than 178,000,000 items, including substantial collections of government documents and congressional records that remain undigitized. Over successive decades, the Federal Government has significantly expanded its capabilities in intelligence collection, surveillance, data analysis, and information processing, while the vast majority of the Government’s own institutional records—documenting the decisions, operations, and conduct of Federal agencies across the full history of the Republic—remain inaccessible to the public in analog form. This asymmetry between the Government’s growing capacity to collect and analyze information about the citizenry and the citizenry’s limited capacity to access and analyze information about its Government undermines the foundational democratic principle that an informed public is the ultimate check on the exercise of governmental power. If the records of the Federal Government remain in analog form while the Government itself deploys advanced computational tools, the benefits of those tools will accrue disproportionately to those with institutional access, and the public’s capacity to oversee, audit, and understand the operations of its own Government will continue to erode.

(16) The documented record of executive branch conduct in the investigation and non-prosecution of the Epstein Network—including a federal court’s finding that the United States Government violated the Crime Victims’ Rights Act by concealing the existence of a non-prosecution agreement from Epstein’s victims; the abandonment of a fully prepared 60-count indictment after supervisory intervention; an unexplained eleven-month gap in the responsible U.S. Attorney’s incoming emails spanning the critical decision period; the CIA’s classified refusal to confirm or deny records of Epstein’s own alleged affiliation; a partially disclosed FBI cooperation relationship; and the institutional failures of the Bureau of Prisons preceding Epstein’s reported death on August 10, 2019, including the failure of surveillance cameras, the failure of assigned guards, and the falsification of count records—constitutes a pattern across multiple executive branch agencies and multiple administrations that demonstrates the insufficiency of voluntary executive self-compliance with disclosure mandates when those mandates implicate persons and institutions of political significance.

(17) The assassination of Charles J. Kirk, founder and chief executive officer of Turning Point USA, on September 10, 2025, at a public event at Utah Valley University, combined with documented anomalies in forensic evidence—including the inability of the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms, and Explosives to positively match the recovered bullet to the rifle attributed to the sole charged suspect—the alteration of the physical crime scene within one week of the assassination during an active capital murder investigation, credible reports that a former Director of the National Counterterrorism Center was directed to cease investigating potential foreign connections, and documented financial pressure on Kirk from major donors tied to his positions on United States foreign policy, raises questions of sufficient gravity to warrant independent, bipartisan congressional inquiry. The absence of a comprehensive, publicly accountable investigation gives rise to speculation that undermines public confidence in the integrity of law enforcement and the administration of justice.

(18) Congress finds that the harms documented in this Act are not independent phenomena amenable to separate legislative remedies. They are interconnected manifestations of a single structural condition: the progressive, multi-generational consolidation of power, information, capability, and impunity in the executive branch and its allied private interests, and the corresponding erosion of the people’s capacity to know what their government does, to constrain how it acts, and to hold it accountable when it fails or transgresses. Surveillance enables coercion. Information asymmetry prevents accountability. Dark money corrupts representation. Unaccountable coercive force deters dissent. Unregulated technology amplifies each of these dynamics. Addressing any one dimension in isolation leaves the structural condition intact.

(19) The Framers understood this interconnection. The Constitution does not address legislative power in one document, executive power in another, and individual rights in a third. It addresses all of them together because they form a system. This Act is an omnibus measure because the problem it addresses is systemic. The eight titles of this Act—addressing artificial intelligence governance, mass surveillance and privacy, political spending disclosure, domestic military deployment, federal law enforcement training, government records digitization, executive accountability and public disclosure, and the investigation of political violence—are designed to function as an integrated strategy for restoring the structural deterrent upon which democratic self-governance depends.

(20) Congress has successfully employed comprehensive, multi-domain legislative strategies when confronting systemic threats to the Republic. The Reconstruction Amendments and their implementing legislation addressed the interconnected dimensions of slavery and its aftermath. The New Deal legislation addressed the interconnected dimensions of the Great Depression. The Civil Rights Act of 1964, the Voting Rights Act of 1965, and the Fair Housing Act of 1968 addressed the interconnected dimensions of racial discrimination. This Act addresses the interconnected dimensions of a structural power imbalance that, if left unaddressed, will render the constitutional design inoperable regardless of which party holds power.

(b) Purpose.—The purposes of this Act are—

(1) to restore the constitutional balance of power between the Federal Government and the people of the United States by simultaneously addressing the multiple, interconnected dimensions of the structural power asymmetry documented in the findings of this section;

(2) to constrain the executive branch’s capacity to surveil, coerce, and control the civilian population by prohibiting mass commercial surveillance, requiring the divestiture and destruction of population-scale databases, categorically banning psychological profiling of American citizens, and closing the commercial data acquisition pathway through which federal agencies circumvent Fourth Amendment protections;

(3) to regulate the development and deployment of artificial intelligence systems through a comprehensive, structured, and time-bound investigatory process that builds the evidentiary record, institutional capacity, and technical expertise necessary for durable and judicially sustainable regulatory standards, while imposing immediate interim protections against documented harms to children, workers, and the public;

(4) to reform the Insurrection Act by replacing its vague and obsolete provisions with specific triggering conditions, mandatory congressional consultation and approval, meaningful judicial review, prohibitions on private deputization and martial law, and structural safeguards against self-interested presidential abuse;

(5) to establish mandatory minimum training standards for federal immigration enforcement personnel, including evidence-based de-escalation training, constitutional law instruction, and independent review of serious use-of-force incidents, to prevent the deployment of undertrained agents into civilian environments;

(6) to require the disclosure of the true and original sources of money spent to influence Federal elections and judicial nominations, to close loopholes permitting foreign national spending in domestic elections, and to restore the transparency conditions upon which the Supreme Court premised its decision in Citizens United v. FEC;

(7) to authorize and direct the mass digitization of the analog holdings of the National Archives and Records Administration, the Library of Congress, and the Government Publishing Office, so that the people of the United States may apply the same analytical tools to the records of their government that the government applies to information about them;

(8) to mandate the full public disclosure of all records relating to the Epstein Network under independent, non-executive oversight, to provide structural whistleblower protections and Espionage Act immunity for disclosures relating to the Network, and to conduct the independent congressional inquiry into the circumstances of Epstein’s reported death that the executive branch has failed to provide;

(9) to establish an independent, bipartisan commission to examine the circumstances surrounding the assassination of Charlie Kirk, including the integrity of forensic evidence, the scope of the investigation, and the role of foreign financial influence on domestic political organizations; and

(10) to ensure, through all of the foregoing measures, that the government of the United States remains accountable to the people it serves, constrained by the Constitution it is sworn to uphold, and transparent in the exercise of the powers with which a free people have entrusted it.

(c) Relationship Between subtitle A and subtitle B. subtitle A (ASPPEA) governs commercial surveillance data practices and government acquisition of commercial surveillance data. subtitle B (EAJOA) establishes the Judicial Executive Oversight System for monitoring executive branch officials’ communications. Each Section is independently enforceable. The invalidation of any provision of one Section does not affect the validity or enforceability of the other Section. Where a provision of subtitle A is referenced by subtitle B or vice versa, the referenced provision is incorporated by reference and shall be interpreted consistently with the purposes of both Sections.

Sec. 1001. Short Title.

This subtitle may be cited as the "American Surveillance Prohibition and Privacy Enforcement Act" or "ASPPEA."

Sec. 1002. Congressional Findings.

The Congress finds the following:

(1) Commercial data brokers, platform operators, and intelligence-aggregation contractors have assembled databases of unprecedented scale covering hundreds of millions of American citizens — in some cases the majority of the adult population of the United States and billions of individuals worldwide — without those citizens' meaningful knowledge or consent.

(2) These databases constitute three distinct and serious threats to constitutional order, individual civil liberties, and democratic self-governance:

(A) Inference-Based Manipulation: The derivation of psychological profiles, emotional vulnerability assessments, personality models, and political susceptibility scores from behavioral data enables the targeting of individuals with persuasive messaging calibrated to exploit cognitive vulnerabilities. This capability, demonstrated at scale in at least one major United States electoral cycle and documented in multiple foreign electoral contexts, enables private and foreign actors to manipulate democratic processes, political beliefs, and electoral outcomes without the knowledge of those targeted.

(B) Operational Targeting: Comprehensive behavioral baselines documenting individuals' locations, routines, relationships, and daily patterns across multiple unrelated contexts constitute operational targeting packages which may be used to physically surveil, locate, monitor, harass, detain, or harm specific individuals or groups. The harm may be inherent in the raw data itself, independent of any derived inference. A record documenting where a person is, when they are there, and who they associate with may be used as a tool of persecution regardless of whether a psychological model has been applied to it.

(C) Totalizing Coverage: Commercial databases with near-complete coverage of the United States population constitute private surveillance infrastructure. The availability of such databases to governments, foreign intelligence services, criminal organizations, and other powerful actors poses a structural threat to democratic governance. The threat is the existence of the database at population scale, independent of current use or stated purpose.

(3) The documented history of the Federal Bureau of Investigation's COINTELPRO program — exposed in 1971 and comprehensively investigated by the Senate Select Committee to Study Governmental Operations with Respect to Intelligence Activities in 1975 and 1976 — demonstrates that domestic intelligence agencies will, absent categorical legal constraints, use surveillance capabilities to target, disrupt, and suppress constitutionally protected political activity including civil rights organizations, labor movements, antiwar groups, and political parties across the political spectrum. Congressional investigations and subsequent Inspector General reports have documented recurring patterns of domestic intelligence collection extending beyond lawful investigative activity to the surveillance of constitutionally protected political conduct. Congress finds that categorical statutory constraints — rather than internal agency policy, executive order, or case-by-case judicial oversight — are necessary to prevent the recurrence of this structural pattern.

(4) Federal agencies have systematically purchased access to commercial data compilations to acquire information they could not constitutionally compel through warrant, subpoena, or other lawful process. The Supreme Court's holding in Carpenter v. United States, 585 U.S. 296 (2018), establishes that this circumvention strategy is constitutionally impermissible; this subtitle extends and codifies that principle concretely.

(5) The use of commercial data infrastructure by foreign intelligence services to conduct influence operations against American citizens — including using psychological profiling to design and target campaigns intended to polarize and manipulate the American electorate — constitutes a direct national security threat requiring the elimination of the commercial data infrastructure on which such operations depend.

(6) Congress finds that a private database covering ten million or more United States persons constitutes a surveillance infrastructure whose scale is incompatible with democratic governance and individual liberty regardless of the purpose for which it was assembled. Congress selects ten million as the threshold because it approximates the population of a large United States state, exceeds three percent of the total adult population, and constitutes a scale sufficient to enable population-level statistical inference, behavioral pattern analysis, and targeted identification of individuals within any demographic, geographic, or political subgroup — capabilities that are qualitatively distinct from those available at smaller scales and that create the structural risks documented in this subtitle’s findings. Once assembled, such a database creates an irreversible capability for population-scale surveillance, targeting, and social control. This finding, grounded in the empirical record of database misuse, foreign intelligence exploitation, and government circumvention of constitutional protections documented throughout this subtitle's findings, supports the structural prohibition of Part G. No commercial justification is proportionate to this structural risk.

(7) Congress finds that ordinary commercial data collection for the purpose of providing relevant goods and services to consumers — including first-party transactional data, contextual advertising, and explicitly declared consumer preferences — serves legitimate purposes and is not the target of this subtitle. The target is the transformation of consumer behavioral data into instruments of psychological manipulation, operational persecution, and population-scale surveillance or to enable the risk thereof.

(8) Congress exercises its authority under Article I of the Constitution, the enforcement clauses of the Fourth and Fourteenth Amendments, and its plenary authority over interstate commerce.

(9) The Federal Trade Commission, in its Final Report "Protecting Consumer Privacy in an Era of Rapid Change: Recommendations for Businesses and Policymakers" (March 2012), formally found that the notice-and-choice model — the framework under which commercial data collection purports to rest on consumer consent — produced long, incomprehensible privacy policies that consumers typically do not read, let alone understand, and that industry self-regulation under this framework had failed to provide adequate and meaningful protection. The FTC called on Congress to enact targeted legislation addressing data broker practices and documented that take-it-or-leave-it consent raises fundamental concerns about the absence of genuine consumer choice for services that have become essential to modern life. Peer-reviewed research published through 2023 confirms that the situation has materially worsened since the FTC's findings: privacy policies are now significantly longer and less comprehensible than they were when the FTC issued its report, average a reading level significantly above the United States adult average, and privacy regulations intended to improve transparency have paradoxically produced longer and less comprehensible policies rather than clearer ones. Congress finds that purported consent obtained through these mechanisms does not constitute meaningful, informed, or voluntary consent, and that databases assembled through such purported consent do not rest on a legitimate property foundation entitling their holders to full compensation upon the divestiture required by this subtitle.

(10) The documented commercial deployment of psychological profiling operations against United States persons — including the operation conducted by Cambridge Analytica and its affiliates, which harvested psychological profile data on tens of millions of Americans from a major social media platform without their knowledge, and used those profiles to design and target political messaging in multiple United States electoral cycles — demonstrates that the commercial availability of psychological profiling infrastructure constitutes a direct and documented threat to democratic self-governance. This operation, and others of similar design, exploited the absence of categorical legal prohibitions on psychological profiling to transform consumer behavioral data into instruments of electoral manipulation. Congress finds that the commercial pathway through which psychological profiling data reaches political actors in these operations must be categorically closed, and that no distinction between commercial and political uses of psychological profiling data is technically or legally meaningful because the same model and the same data can serve both functions.

Sec. 1003. Definitions.

As used in this subtitle:

(1) "Covered Personal Data" means any information, alone or in combination, that identifies or is reasonably linkable to a specific individual or a device associated with that individual, including: precise or coarse geolocation data; financial transaction records; communications metadata; biometric data; health or medical data; political affiliation or activity; religious belief or practice; social or associational graph data; device identifiers; audio, video, or visual data from a user's device; keystroke or input data; and inferred or derived characteristics generated by algorithmic processing of any of the foregoing.

(2) "Psychological Profile" means any data compilation, model, score, index, or derived output that assesses, estimates, or predicts any of the following concerning a specific individual: personality characteristics, including any model based on the OCEAN framework or equivalent taxonomy; emotional state, mood, or affective condition; cognitive vulnerabilities or susceptibility to persuasion; political beliefs, affiliations, or likely responses to political messaging; religious belief or spiritual orientation; or behavioral responses to persuasive, emotional, or ideological stimuli. A Psychological Profile is prohibited regardless of the accuracy of its assessments and regardless of whether it is derived from individually innocuous inputs.

(3) "Operational Profile" means any data asset, system, or query capability that, when executed, is capable of producing a comprehensive behavioral record of a specific individual’s locations, routines, associational contacts, relationship network, or behavioral patterns derived from two or more independent contextual sources. “Independent contextual sources” means data from unrelated commercial or operational contexts — such as retail purchase data, mobile location data, and communications metadata — as distinguished from multiple data points arising from a single direct commercial relationship. The detailed scope of this prohibition, including the capability standard and the prohibition on technical evasion, is specified in Sec. 1031 of this subtitle.

(4) "Population Coverage Database" means any database, data system, or combination of linked data assets containing Covered Personal Data, Psychological Profiles, or Operational Profiles concerning more than ten million (10,000,000) United States persons, or concerning more than three percent (3%) of the population of any United States state, territory, or metropolitan statistical area, whichever threshold is smaller.

(5) "Precrime Risk Assessment" means any score, index, classification, or predictive output assessing the likelihood that a specific individual will engage in criminal, violent, politically disruptive, or socially deviant conduct, based on behavioral, demographic, associational, or predictive data rather than evidence of a specific past act. The term does not include the systems exempted under Sec. 1021(c).

(6) "Data Broker" means any person or entity engaged in the business of collecting, compiling, aggregating, inferring, licensing, selling, or otherwise providing access to Covered Personal Data concerning individuals with whom the entity has no direct commercial relationship, for monetary or other valuable consideration, regardless of commercial designation.

(7) “Data-Monetizing Collector” means any entity with a direct commercial relationship with consumers that also derives revenue from the sale, licensing, or transfer of Covered Personal Data to third parties, for monetary or other valuable consideration. Entities are classified into Tier DM-1, Tier DM-2, and Tier DM-3 based on the percentage of relevant gross revenues derived from such third-party transfers, as specified in Sec. 1113A of this subtitle. Entities deriving less than five percent (5%) of relevant gross revenues from third-party data transfers are eligible for the safe harbor of Sec. 1081 provided all other safe harbor conditions are met. The tiered structure governs registration, audit, and enforcement tier classification only, not the scope of the substantive prohibitions.

(8) "Targeting Infrastructure Contractor" means any person or entity that builds, operates, maintains, or provides analytical, technical, or platform services used to produce Psychological Profiles, Operational Profiles, Precrime Risk Assessments, or Population Coverage Databases, whether on behalf of a government entity, political organization, commercial advertiser, or any other principal.

(9) "Federal Agency" means any executive department, independent agency, military department, government corporation, or other establishment in the executive branch, including any office within the Executive Office of the President, and any independent regulatory agency.

(10) "Government Contractor" means any person or entity acting under contract, grant, cooperative agreement, or any other arrangement with or on behalf of a Federal Agency, to the extent such person or entity collects, processes, accesses, or transfers Covered Personal Data in connection with such arrangement.

(11) "Constitutionally Equivalent Collection" means collection a Federal Agency could lawfully accomplish through direct legal process supported by probable cause and authorized by a court of competent jurisdiction, or through the explicit, revocable, and informed consent of the data subject.

(12) "Foreign Direction" means operational direction, material funding, or strategic coordination received by an organization or individual from a foreign government, foreign intelligence service, or their knowing agents. "Material funding" means financial support that:

(A) constitutes more than ten percent (10%) of the recipient organization's total annual operating budget in any twelve-month period; or

(B) is conditioned, expressly or impliedly, on specific organizational conduct, the adoption of specific positions, the targeting of specific individuals or groups, or the achievement of specific outcomes; or

(C) is structured in whole or in part to conceal its foreign government origin through intermediary entities, shell organizations, or nominee transactions. The following do not constitute Foreign Direction: expressions of public support by a foreign government for a domestic organization's positions; receipt of funding not meeting the materiality criteria above; participation in international civil society networks or transnational advocacy coalitions; solidarity relationships with foreign organizations engaged in parallel domestic activities; or acceptance of grants from foreign-based philanthropic foundations acting independently of any government. Arm's-length commercial transactions at fair market value — including retail sales of merchandise, publications, event tickets, and advertising — do not constitute "funding" regardless of the identity of the purchaser, and shall not be counted toward the materiality threshold of subparagraph (A). A pattern of commercial transactions that appears designed to manufacture a Foreign Direction predicate triggers the foreign interference response mechanism of Sec. 1073 and may not be used as a Stage One predicate under Sec. 1072.

(13) "First Amendment Organization" means any organization whose stated mission and primary operational conduct consists of constitutionally protected activity under the First Amendment, including political organizing, electoral advocacy, public interest litigation, journalism, labor organizing, environmental or social justice activism, religious practice and community organization, and academic research and publication. The commercial revenue, commercial activities, or commercial organizational structures of an organization are irrelevant to this determination. An organization qualifies regardless of whether it also engages in revenue generating activities, provided those activities are incidental to and in service of its primary First Amendment mission.

(14) "Minimization Procedures" means documented policies and technical controls limiting acquisition, retention, use, and dissemination of Covered Personal Data to the minimum necessary to accomplish the specific authorized purpose, with mandatory purging of nonqualifying data within ninety (90) days.

(15) "Sensitive Nation-State Actor" means any foreign government, state-owned enterprise, or entity in which a foreign government holds a controlling interest, incorporated under the laws of a country designated as a Foreign Adversary under 15 C.F.R. § 7.4.

(16) "United States-Directed Entity" means any person or entity incorporated or domiciled outside the United States that: directs its data collection, platform services, or data brokerage activities at United States persons; processes data of United States persons on systems accessible from or managed from within the United States; or derives more than twenty percent (20%) of its gross revenues from activities involving the data of United States persons. A United StatesDirected Entity is subject to this subtitle with respect to its activities involving the data of United States persons to the same extent as an entity incorporated within the United States.

(17) "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission.

(18) "Bureau" means the Bureau of Data Surveillance established under Sec. 1111.

(19) “Data Cooperative” means any arrangement, agreement, or platform through which two or more entities pool, exchange, contribute, or provide reciprocal access to Covered Personal Data concerning individuals with whom at least one participating entity has no direct commercial relationship, regardless of whether consideration is exchanged in the form of money, data reciprocity, platform access, or any other benefit. The term includes any arrangement in which an entity receives data about individuals it did not directly collect in exchange for providing data about individuals it did collect, regardless of how the arrangement is commercially designated.

(20) “Audience-Segment Licensing Arrangement” means any arrangement through which an entity makes available to a third party — whether through direct license, platform access, API, programmatic advertising infrastructure, or any other mechanism — a defined group or cohort of individuals selected on the basis of Covered Personal Data, inferred characteristics, behavioral attributes, or any combination thereof, for the purpose of enabling the third party to direct communications, advertising, or messaging at those individuals, regardless of whether individual-level data is formally transferred or only targeting access is provided. The functional test is whether the arrangement enables a third party to reach a defined population selected on the basis of Covered Personal Data — not whether data physically moves between parties.

(21) “Contextual Advertising Signal” means Covered Personal Data processed in real time from the specific content a user is currently viewing or the specific query a user has currently submitted — including the subject matter, category, keywords, or semantic content of the webpage, article, video, application, or search query currently being consumed or submitted — that is used solely to select advertising relevant to that specific content or query. A Contextual Advertising Signal is Covered Personal Data within the meaning of Sec. 1003(1). The qualifying conditions, permitted uses, and clean-inputs rule applicable to Contextual Advertising Signals are specified in Sec. 1091A of this subtitle.

(22) “Session” means, for purposes of advertising data retention under this subtitle, the period beginning when a user initiates an interaction with a platform, application, or service and ending upon the earliest of: explicit user logout; application or browser tab close; thirty (30) minutes of continuous user inactivity; or twenty-four (24) hours from initiation. The detailed rules governing Session termination, active service delivery, multi-device sessions, the implementation safe harbor, and the relationship of Sessions to stored user content are specified in Sec. 1094A of this subtitle.

(23) “Small Business” means any person or entity that satisfies all three of the following criteria as of the most recent calendar year preceding the date on which qualification is assessed:

(A) Global annual revenues — meaning revenues from all sources and all geographic markets, including the global annual revenues of all affiliated entities as determined under the affiliation rules of this paragraph — of less than forty million dollars ($40,000,000);

(B) Collection, processing, or transfer of Covered Personal Data concerning no more than two hundred thousand (200,000) United States persons, counting each individual once regardless of how many times their data was processed, aggregated across the entity and all affiliated entities; and

(C) No more than twenty-five percent (25%) of global annual revenues, calculated across the entity and all affiliated entities, derived from the transfer of Covered Personal Data to third parties who do not have a direct commercial relationship with the individuals whose data is transferred. Where the Bureau has published final rules defining “relevant gross revenues” under Sec. 1003(7), the percentage in this criterion shall be calculated using the same revenue base as those rules specify. An entity satisfying all three criteria is a Small Business regardless of its legal form. Affiliation: For purposes of criteria (A), (B), and (C), all revenues, data holdings, and data transfer activities of the following entities are aggregated with those of the entity being assessed: (i) any entity in which the assessed entity holds a direct or indirect ownership interest of ten percent (10%) or more; (ii) any entity that holds a direct or indirect ownership interest of ten percent (10%) or more in the assessed entity; (iii) any entity under common ownership or common operational control with the assessed entity, where operational control means the practical ability to direct, limit, set terms for, or access the data practices of the other entity through contract, platform architecture, board representation, management overlap, or shared technical infrastructure regardless of formal legal separation; (iv) any entity with which the assessed entity participates in a Data Cooperative as defined in Sec. 1003(19) or an Audience-Segment Licensing Arrangement as defined in Sec. 1003(20), to the extent of the Covered Personal Data accessible through that arrangement; and (v) any entity to which the assessed entity has transferred Covered Personal Data within the preceding twenty-four (24) months where that transfer was structured in whole or in part to reduce the assessed entity’s record count below a threshold of this subtitle. The Bureau shall promulgate rules specifying the methodology for aggregating records and revenues across affiliated entities within one hundred eighty (180) days after the Director’s first day of service.

(24) “Explicit User-Declared Preference” means a preference for a content category, topic, product type, brand, service category, or specific content source that a user has affirmatively expressed through deliberate, conscious action, including: structured preference declarations through a dedicated preference interface; follows and subscriptions to specific accounts, creators, channels, communities, or content sources; saves and bookmarks of specific content; category-level like patterns accumulated within an identifiable content category; and deliberate content initiation by clicking, tapping, or navigating to specific content. Passive behavioral engagement signals — including scroll behavior, hover time, view duration of autoplayed content, near-clicks, and autoplay continuations — are not Explicit User-Declared Preferences. The conditions governing use, retention, platform-specificity, recalibration, and intra-category diversity applicable to Explicit User-Declared Preferences are specified in Sec. 1084 of this subtitle.

(25) “Bureau-approved independent technical auditor” means, following the establishment of the Bureau’s auditor certification program under Sec. 1116(c), an independent technical auditor holding current certification under that program. Prior to the establishment of that program, “Bureau-approved independent technical auditor” means an independent technical auditor that:

(A) has no financial interest in and has not been employed by the entity being audited within the preceding five (5) years;

(B) possesses demonstrated expertise in data systems architecture, privacy compliance, and the specific technical subject matter of the audit being conducted; and

(C) has been approved by the Bureau of Consumer Protection acting as interim enforcement authority under Sec. 1152(1), or, where the Bureau of Consumer Protection has published interim auditor qualification standards within sixty (60) days of enactment, meets those published standards without requiring individual approval. The Bureau of Consumer Protection shall publish interim auditor qualification standards within sixty (60) days of enactment. An auditor meeting these requirements is deemed a Bureau-approved independent technical auditor for all purposes of this subtitle until the Bureau’s formal certification program under Sec. 1116(c) is operational, at which point auditors not certified within one (1) year of the program’s establishment are no longer deemed approved under this transitional provision.

Part A — Prohibition on Psychological Profiles and Behavioral Prediction

Sec. 1011. Absolute Prohibition on Psychological Profiles.

(a) No entity subject to the jurisdiction of the United States shall compile, derive, produce, sell, license, transfer, or use a Psychological Profile concerning any United States person for any purpose. Without limiting the foregoing categorical prohibition, this prohibition expressly covers: the use of a Psychological Profile to target, select, or tailor advertising, political messaging, electoral communications, voter targeting, influence operations, or any other commercial, political, or persuasive communication directed at a specific individual; the construction or operation of services designed to produce Psychological Profiles on behalf of any third party, including any government entity, political organization, commercial advertiser, foreign government, or influence operation; and the sale, license, transfer, or provision of any Psychological Profile, Operational Profile, or Precrime Risk Assessment to any political campaign, political action committee, super PAC, political party, electoral organization, ballot initiative committee, or any entity primarily engaged in electoral or political advocacy, regardless of whether such Profile is used for voter targeting, opposition research, persuasion messaging, or any other electoral purpose.

(b) This prohibition is absolute. No commercial purpose, national security justification, or data subject consent renders the compilation or use of a Psychological Profile lawful under this

Section. The prohibition applies regardless of the accuracy of the Profile and regardless of whether it is derived from individually innocuous inputs.

(c) Academic Research Carve-Out. Notwithstanding subsections (b) and (d), the prohibition in subsection (a) does not apply to the compilation or analysis of Psychological Profiles conducted exclusively for bona fide academic research purposes, subject to all of the following conditions:

(1) the research is approved by an Institutional Review Board constituted under 45 C.F.R. Part 46 or an equivalent accredited ethics review body;

(2) the research is conducted exclusively on synthetic data — data generated algorithmically to replicate statistical properties of real data without corresponding to any actual individual — or on fully anonymized data that has been processed through a technically verified de-identification procedure such that re-identification of any specific individual is not reasonably possible;

(3) no Psychological Profile, model weights, trained parameters, scoring algorithm, or other artifact capable of producing Psychological Profile outputs, nor any underlying behavioral data constituting an Operational Profile, may be sold, licensed, transferred, shared, or disclosed to any commercial entity, government entity, political organization, or any third party other than through peer-reviewed academic publication of aggregate statistical findings that do not enable reconstruction of any individual profile or scoring model;

(4) the existence and general subject matter of the research is disclosed to the Bureau annually; and

(5) any Psychological Profile, model weights, trained parameters, scoring algorithm, underlying behavioral data, and any other artifact capable of producing Psychological Profile outputs shall be permanently and irreversibly destroyed upon completion of the research project and no later than three (3) years from the date of IRB approval. The carve-out of this subsection extends to cross-context behavioral data constituting an Operational Profile assembled incidentally in the course of permitted psychological profiling research, provided such data meets all conditions of this subsection including the destruction requirement. The carve-out does not extend to datasets meeting the Population Coverage Database threshold of Sec. 1003(4). A researcher who uses this carve-out to produce, retain, or transfer a Psychological Profile, model, or underlying dataset for any commercial, political, or government purpose is subject to the full civil and criminal penalties of this subtitle as if the carve-out did not apply, and such conduct constitutes a per se Tier 3 willful violation. The Bureau shall promulgate standards for technically verified de-identification procedures eligible for use under this carve-out within one hundred eighty (180) days after the Director's first day of service, through notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553. Until such standards are final, de-identification under this carve-out shall satisfy the requirements of NIST Special Publication 800-188 (De-Identifying Government Datasets) or a successor standard designated by the Bureau, as the minimum applicable baseline. De-identification verification shall be certified by a Bureau-approved independent technical auditor, and the certification shall be submitted to the Bureau along with the annual disclosure required by condition (4) of this subsection.

(d) Clean-Inputs Rule. A Psychological Profile is prohibited regardless of whether its component data inputs are individually drawn from otherwise permitted categories. The test is the nature and purpose of the output, not the permissibility of the inputs. An entity that feeds only permitted first-party data into a model producing a personality score, emotional vulnerability index, or political susceptibility score has produced a prohibited Psychological Profile.

(d)(2) Permitted Output Characteristics. An output of an algorithmic system does not constitute a Psychological Profile solely by reason of being generated by a system that also receives as inputs data categories described in the Psychological Profile definition, where the output satisfies all of the following conditions:

(A) the output is limited to a binary or categorical determination of whether specific content, product, or service falls within a category a user has explicitly declared interest in through an Explicit User-Declared Preference meeting all conditions of this subtitle, without generating any score, index, or ranking predicting the user’s emotional or psychological response to that specific content, product, or service;

(B) the output does not distinguish between two pieces of content within the same user-declared category on the basis of any predicted differential in emotional impact, psychological engagement, outrage potential, persuasion susceptibility, or behavioral response specific to the individual user; and

(C) the output is not combined with any signal, score, or inference concerning the user’s current or predicted emotional state, psychological condition, or cognitive vulnerability.

(d)(3) Prohibited Output Characteristics. Notwithstanding the permissibility of any input, an output constitutes a Psychological Profile and is subject to the absolute prohibition of subsection (a) where:

(A) the output ranks, scores, sequences, or selects content, products, services, or messaging for a specific individual user based on a model predicting that user’s emotional engagement, psychological response, re-engagement likelihood, or behavioral response to that specific content, product, service, or messaging;

(B) the output includes any score, index, or signal predicting a specific individual user’s susceptibility to persuasion, emotional arousal, psychological captivation, or behavioral response to specific content or messaging; or

(C) the output, if disclosed to a third party, would enable that third party to calibrate content, products, services, or messaging to exploit that specific individual user’s predicted psychological responses, emotional state, or cognitive vulnerabilities.

(d)(4) Objective Function Compliance Test. For purposes of determining whether a content recommendation system’s objective function produces a prohibited output under subsection (d)(3), an entity shall apply the following two-part test:

(A) Substitution Test. The objective function is permitted where, if applied to two pieces of content that a user has explicitly declared interest in through Explicit User-Declared Preferences, the function would rank those pieces of content identically regardless of any differential in their predicted emotional impact on that specific user. The objective function is prohibited where it would rank one above the other based on any predicted differential in emotional response, psychological engagement, or persuasion susceptibility specific to that user.

(B) Category Collapse Test. An objective function that produces, over any thirty (30) day period for any given user, a pattern of recommendations in which the user receives content from fewer than three

(3) distinct subcategories within any single content category in which that user has declared interest is presumptively prohibited as producing a Psychological Profile through systematic engagement optimization. The entity may rebut this presumption by demonstrating through a Bureau-approved independent technical auditor that the category concentration resulted from the user’s own Explicit User-Declared Preferences rather than from algorithmic optimization.

(d)(5) Annual Objective Function Certification. Any entity operating a content recommendation system on a platform with more than one million (1,000,000) monthly active United States users shall certify annually to the Bureau, within thirty (30) days of the close of each calendar year, that its recommendation system objective function satisfies the Substitution Test of subsection (d)(4)

(A) and has not produced a prohibited Category Collapse pattern under subsection (d)(4)

(B) during the preceding calendar year. The certification shall be prepared by a Bureau-approved independent technical auditor, shall describe the auditor’s methodology and test results in sufficient detail to permit Bureau verification, and shall be signed under penalty of perjury by both the entity’s Chief Executive Officer and Chief Privacy Officer or designated responsible officer.

(e) Mental Health Application Exemption. The absolute prohibition of subsection (a) does not apply to the compilation or processing of data that would otherwise constitute a Psychological Profile where such compilation or processing is conducted exclusively by or under the clinical supervision of a licensed mental health professional, licensed healthcare entity, or accredited behavioral health organization operating a mental health application that satisfies all conditions of Sec. 1021(c)(8). A mental health application that satisfies all conditions of Sec. 1021(c)(8) is exempt from the prohibition of this subtitle to the extent of the data compiled or processed within the therapeutic relationship for the purpose of providing the specific mental health service for which the user enrolled. The exemption of this subsection is strictly co-extensive with the conditions of Sec. 1021(c)(8): any violation of those conditions — including any third-party transfer in violation of Sec. 1021(c)(8)(B) — simultaneously strips this exemption and subjects the entity to the full prohibition of subsection (a) and the Tier 3 penalties specified in Sec. 1021(c)(8)(E).

Part B — Prohibition on Precrime Risk Assessments

Sec. 1021. Prohibition on Precrime Risk Assessments.

(a) No entity subject to the jurisdiction of the United States shall compile, derive, produce, sell, license, transfer, or use a Precrime Risk Assessment concerning any United States person for any purpose, including law enforcement, insurance, employment, credit, housing, or national security.

(b) No Federal Agency, state or local government entity, or Government Contractor shall purchase, license, or use a Precrime Risk Assessment from any commercial source, or direct or fund the development of a Precrime Risk Assessment system through any contract, grant, or cooperative agreement.

(c) Exempted Systems. The prohibition in subsection (a) does not apply to:

(1) actuarial risk assessment tools used in criminal sentencing or parole proceedings based solely on an individual's own documented criminal history, as distinguished from predictive behavioral models based on demographic, associational, or population-level data;

(2) consumer credit scoring models and risk assessments regulated under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, 15 U.S.C. § 1681 et seq., provided such models are not sold to, licensed to, or used by any Federal Agency for any purpose other than the evaluation of a Federal credit application or a background check specifically authorized by statute;

(3) actuarial models used by licensed insurance carriers to price insurance products based on statistically validated risk factors for the specific product category, provided such models are not used to deny coverage based on political beliefs, religious affiliation, or protected characteristics, and are not sold or licensed to any government entity;

(4) fraud detection and anti-money-laundering systems operated by financial institutions in compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act and applicable FinCEN regulations, provided such systems are operated solely within the direct commercial relationship between the financial institution and its own customers and are not sold, licensed, or transferred to any commercial third party; government disclosure is permitted only as required by applicable law, including mandatory Suspicious Activity Report filing with the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network under 31 U.S.C. § 5318(g), responses to grand jury subpoena, and compliance with court orders — but does not extend to voluntary transfers to any law enforcement agency or government entity beyond what applicable law specifically requires;

(5) medical risk stratification models used by licensed healthcare providers to allocate clinical resources and provide preventive care, operated solely within the direct treatment relationship and not transferred to any government entity or commercial data broker;

(6) transaction fraud detection systems used by payment processors and merchants to identify fraudulent transactions in real time, provided such systems assess only the specific transaction and are not used to build persistent behavioral profiles of individual consumers;

(7) First Step Act Risk and Needs Assessment — PATTERN. The Bureau of Prisons' Prisoner Assessment Tool Targeting Estimated Risk and Needs (PATTERN), mandated under the First Step Act of 2018, 18 U.S.C. § 3632, is exempt from the prohibition of subsection (a). PATTERN serves legitimate correctional functions — including the allocation of rehabilitative programming, the assessment of recidivism risk for reentry placement decisions, and the determination of appropriate supervision levels — that directly benefit the individuals assessed and serve the correctional purposes for which the Bureau of Prisons is responsible. Congress does not find that PATTERN constitutes a Precrime Risk Assessment within the meaning of Sec. 1003(5) with respect to its use within the federal correctional system for these purposes, because PATTERN operates on individuals already convicted and sentenced through constitutional criminal process, for the purpose of allocating correctional services and making reentry determinations, and not for the purpose of predicting future criminal conduct among the general population.

(A) Permitted Uses. PATTERN may be used for: rehabilitative programming allocation including education, vocational training, substance abuse treatment, and cognitive behavioral programming; reentry placement decisions including halfway house placement and home confinement eligibility; community supervision level determinations; and any other purpose specifically authorized by the First Step Act of 2018.

(B) Prohibited Uses. PATTERN scores and underlying data may not be used for any purpose constituting predictive policing — meaning any decision to investigate, surveil, stop, search, or target an individual who is not already subject to a pending criminal proceeding or an existing term of federal supervision. PATTERN scores may not be the sole or primary basis for denying supervised release or home confinement without individualized review by a qualified case manager or court. PATTERN data and scores may be disclosed to courts and probation officers in connection with sentencing, supervised release, and violation proceedings involving the assessed individual; to interstate compact supervision agencies receiving a released individual; to sex offender registration authorities for tier determination purposes; to federally coordinated reentry services providers for the purpose of matching released individuals with appropriate community services; and to subsequent federal prosecutors in connection with sentencing of a previously assessed individual, through the presentence investigation report process. PATTERN data and scores may not be sold, licensed, or transferred to any commercial entity, any foreign government, or any law enforcement agency for use in investigations unrelated to an existing proceeding involving the specific assessed individual.

(C) Racial Disparity Transparency Requirement. Congress finds that independent validation studies have documented racial and ethnic disparities in PATTERN score distributions that are not fully explained by individual criminal history factors alone, and that these disparities are a matter of ongoing congressional concern. The Director of the Bureau of Prisons shall transmit to Congress annually, beginning one year after enactment, a report documenting: PATTERN score distributions disaggregated by race, ethnicity, offense category, and facility type; the correlation between PATTERN scores and actual recidivism outcomes disaggregated by the same demographic categories; any modifications made to the PATTERN model during the preceding year and their effect on score distributions; and the Director's assessment of whether the observed disparities are attributable to individual criminal history factors, demographic proxy variables, or systemic factors outside the correctional system. This report shall be transmitted to the Senate Committee on the Judiciary, the House Committee on the Judiciary, the Senate Committee on Appropriations Subcommittee on Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies, and the House Committee on Appropriations Subcommittee on Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies.

(D) Congressional Direction for Subsequent Legislation. It is the sense of Congress that the racial and ethnic disparities documented in PATTERN score distributions represent a serious concern requiring ongoing attention, study, and legislative action. Congress directs the National Institute of Justice, in coordination with the Bureau of Prisons, to conduct a comprehensive study of whether PATTERN's predictive accuracy can be maintained while reducing racial and ethnic disparities in score distributions, and to transmit findings and recommendations to Congress not later than two years after enactment. These findings shall inform subsequent legislation addressing the design, validation, and oversight of risk assessment tools used in the federal correctional system. Nothing in this paragraph requires the Bureau of Prisons to modify or replace PATTERN prior to such subsequent legislation; and

(8) Licensed Mental Health Application Exemption. A mental health application operated by or under the clinical supervision of a licensed mental health professional, licensed healthcare entity, or accredited behavioral health organization may collect and process data concerning a user's emotional state, psychological condition, mood, or behavioral patterns solely within the direct therapeutic or supportive relationship with that user, subject to all of the following conditions:

(A) the data is used exclusively to provide the specific mental health service for which the user enrolled and for no other purpose;

(B) the data may not under any circumstances be sold, licensed, transferred, or made accessible to any third party, including any data broker, advertiser, employer, insurer, law enforcement agency, government entity, or foreign person — this prohibition is absolute and no user consent purports to authorize such transfer;

(C) the application shall implement security standards no less protective than those required by the HIPAA Security Rule, 45 C.F.R. Part 164, Subpart C, regardless of whether the application is a covered entity under HIPAA — this requirement establishes an independent statutory floor applicable to all mental health applications under this paragraph, and compliance with HIPAA by a covered entity satisfies this requirement but does not limit the obligation of non-covered entities to meet the same standard;

(D) the application must obtain explicit, informed, separately executed written consent — not bundled within general terms of service or end-user license agreements — disclosing in plain language what specific data is collected, how it is used within the therapeutic relationship, and the absolute prohibition on third-party transfer;

(E) a mental health application that violates the third-party transfer prohibition of subparagraph

(B) is subject to Tier 3 civil penalties under Sec. 1122(e) and the criminal penalties of Sec. 1123, and a violation of subparagraph

(B) is a per se willful violation not eligible for the Tier 1 reduction of Sec. 1122(a); and

(F) the Commission, in consultation with the Department of Health and Human Services, shall promulgate minimum technical security standards for mental health applications under this paragraph within one hundred eighty (180) days of enactment, which shall supplement and not replace applicable HIPAA requirements.

(d) The exemptions in subsection (c) do not apply where any exempted system is: sold, licensed, or transferred to any government entity at any level — federal, state, local, tribal, or territorial — including any administrative agency, regulatory body, benefits agency, housing authority, child welfare agency, or public health agency, except as specifically authorized by subsections (c)(1) and (c)(7), or to any foreign entity; used to make decisions affecting individuals outside the specific commercial or clinical relationship for which the model was developed; or combined with data from external non-relationship sources — meaning data from sources outside the specific commercial or clinical relationship, including data brokers, behavioral data, location data, or any other Covered Personal Data not originating from the direct relationship between the entity and the individual — to produce outputs extending beyond the specific function for which the exemption is claimed. For purposes of the credit scoring exemption in subsection (c)(2), the combination of data from multiple credit data furnishers reporting within the FCRA ecosystem does not constitute combination with "external sources" under this subsection, as such combination is inherent to the consumer reporting function authorized by the FCRA.

Part B-1 — Child Welfare Predictive Risk Assessment — Sense of Congress and Commission

Sec. 1022. Child Welfare Predictive Risk Assessment Tools — Sense of Congress and Commission.

(a) Sense of Congress. It is the sense of Congress that:

(1) Child welfare agencies at the state and local level have deployed predictive risk assessment tools — including tools such as the Allegheny Family Screening Tool and comparable instruments used in jurisdictions across the country — for the purpose of assisting caseworkers in identifying children at risk of maltreatment. Congress recognizes that these tools serve a compelling child protection purpose and that structured risk assessment, when combined with professional human judgment, may improve child welfare outcomes compared to unstructured caseworker discretion alone.

(2) Independent research has documented racial and ethnic disparities in the score distributions of child welfare predictive risk tools, arising in part from the use of variables — including public benefits receipt history, public systems involvement, and socioeconomic indicators — that are correlated with race and ethnicity through systemic inequity rather than through individual conduct or documented risk. Congress finds these disparities are a matter of serious concern that warrants rigorous federal study and subsequent legislative attention.

(3) The resolution of this tension — between the child protection value of structured risk assessment and the racial equity harms of demographic proxy variables — requires empirical study, engagement with affected communities, and careful legislative design. This subtitle does not prohibit state and local child welfare agencies from using existing predictive risk assessment tools, and does not impose any funding condition on their continued use, because Congress recognizes that mandating tool replacement without providing transition funding and validated replacement tools would risk harm to the children these tools are designed to protect.
