In conducting Domain Examination, each TWG shall actively seek out available public opinion research and public input bearing on its domain, including outputs of citizens assemblies organized by any person or entity, deliberative polling processes, nationally representative surveys, recorded and structured public debates, public opinion research commissioned by any person or entity, and other mechanisms through which the views of affected populations on AI-related questions have been gathered. Where such research or input is available, it shall be incorporated into the TWG’s evidentiary record with appropriate assessment of the source and methodology. The TWG Administrative Coordinator shall query the shared evidence repository and the Public Engagement Coordinator for relevant public opinion research and input at the outset of the Domain Examination phase and on a rolling basis throughout the investigatory period;

(ii) Intervention Evaluation — evaluating potential legislative interventions and solutions, including their costs, benefits, constitutional parameters, implementation requirements, and enforcement mechanisms. All options shall be evaluated, including no regulation, repeal or modification of existing legislation, and industry self-regulation, as well as new federal regulatory frameworks; no option shall be excluded from consideration on grounds other than the evidentiary record

(iii) Tradeoff Assessment — assessing the tradeoffs among alternatives, including the distributional effects of each approach, the risks of over-regulation and under-regulation, the constitutional vulnerabilities of each option, and the administrative feasibility of implementation

(iv) Recommendation Development — developing specific legislative recommendations with supporting rationale and evidentiary basis, or a formal finding that no regulation is warranted with the specific evidentiary and constitutional basis for that finding. A finding of no regulation is a legitimate and respected output, not a default or a failure.

(M) Communication Log.—The TWG Administrative Coordinator shall maintain a complete, contemporaneous public log of all external communications made in connection with any TWG's work. The log shall include: the date of each communication; the name, title, and organizational affiliation of each external party communicated with; the medium of communication; the TWG or TWG member to whom the communication was directed; and a brief description of the subject matter. The log shall be updated within 3 business days of each communication and published on the public portal under section 9(d). All written communications — including email, direct messages, and any other written medium — sent to or received by any TWG member in their TWG capacity shall be treated as public records and transmitted to the Public Docket Administrator within 5 business days of receipt or transmission. The Public Docket Administrator shall publish all such communications, subject only to redaction of: personally identifiable information of private individuals; information subject to law enforcement privilege; and information submitted under a confidentiality agreement pursuant to section 10(c), provided that the existence of the communication and the identity of the submitting party shall always be disclosed even where content is redacted.

(N) Public Submission Channel.— Each TWG shall maintain a dedicated public-facing email address for that TWG, established and published on the public portal by the Technical Systems Manager not later than Day 120. The email address shall follow a standardized format identifying the TWG by number and domain. The TWG Administrative Coordinator — not any TWG member — shall manage the public submission inbox for their assigned TWG and shall be solely responsible for receiving, logging, triaging, and routing inbound submissions. Any person, organization, researcher, community group, affected individual, or other party may submit written information, evidence, research, analysis, or policy argument to a TWG through the public submission channel. Submissions through the public channel shall be subject to the following requirements and procedures

(i) Conflict of Interest Disclosure: each submission shall include the name and organizational affiliation of the submitting party; a description of any financial relationship between the submitting party and any frontier AI developer, significant AI deployer, or any entity whose primary business involves the development, deployment, or commercialization of AI systems or any entity with a material financial interest in the TWG's domain within the preceding one year; and a disclosure of whether the submission was prepared in whole or in part by a person with a financial interest in the TWG's recommendations. The TWG Administrative Coordinator shall not route a submission to TWG members until required disclosures are complete; submissions without required disclosures shall be returned with a request for completion within 5 business days. (ii) Logging and Publication: all submissions received through the public channel shall be logged in the communication log within 3 business days of receipt and published on the public docket within 5 business days of receipt, together with the accompanying conflict of interest disclosure. No submission shall be withheld from the public docket except on the specific grounds authorized by subsection (e)(2)(M). (iii) Routing: the TWG Administrative Coordinator shall route each submission to the TWG member or members whose expertise is most relevant to the subject matter of the submission, with a copy to the TWG Chair. The TWG Chair shall determine whether the submission warrants a response, referral to a subgroup, consideration at the weekly consolidation session, or no further action. The TWG is not required to respond to or act on any particular submission, but shall not exclude submissions from its consideration on any basis other than relevance to its mandate. (iv) Volume Management: if the volume of public submissions to a particular TWG exceeds the Administrative Coordinator's capacity to process within required timeframes, the Administrative Coordinator shall notify the Executive Director, who shall provide additional administrative support from the broader FAC staff or from GAO staff under the interagency agreement.

(O) Expert Presentations.— TWGs shall actively solicit expert presentations as a primary method of evidence gathering throughout the investigatory period. Expert presentations are internal working sessions of each TWG, closed to public attendance under subsection (e)(2)(G)(1). The following procedures govern expert presentations

(i) TWG-Initiated Presentations: any TWG member may nominate an outside expert to present to the TWG by submitting a written recommendation to the TWG Chair identifying the proposed presenter, their relevant expertise, and the specific subject matter on which the presentation is sought. The TWG Chair shall accept or decline the nomination within 5 days. If accepted, the TWG Administrative Coordinator shall contact the proposed presenter and schedule the presentation. (ii) Publicly Requested Presentations: any person or organization that wishes to present to a specific TWG may submit a presentation request through the TWG’s public submission channel. The request shall identify the proposed presenter, their relevant expertise, and the specific subject matter and argument they wish to present. The TWG Chair shall accept or decline the request within 5 days of receipt by the Administrative Coordinator. The TWG Chair’s decision is final and is not subject to appeal, but the grounds for declining shall be documented in writing and published on the public docket. Requests may be declined on the grounds that the subject matter falls outside the TWG’s domain mandate, that the presentation would be duplicative of evidence already received, or that the TWG’s schedule does not permit additional presentations given the 8.5-month deadline — but may not be declined on the basis of the presenter’s viewpoint or the expected content of the presentation. (iii) Scheduling: all accepted presentations shall be scheduled by the TWG Administrative Coordinator. Presentations may be conducted during the weekly all-day consolidation session or during a separately scheduled working session. (iv) Presenter Disclosure Requirements: each presenter shall, before presenting, complete the conflict-of-interest disclosure required for public submissions under subparagraph (N)(i). The disclosure shall be published on the public docket within 5 business days of the presentation. (v) Public Log Entry: the TWG Administrative Coordinator shall create a public log entry for each expert presentation within 3 business days of the session. The log entry shall include: the date of the presentation; the name, title, and organizational affiliation of the presenter; the subject matter of the presentation at a level of specificity sufficient to describe the topic presented; and the TWG to which the presentation was made. The content of the presentation — including slides, documents provided, and the discussion that followed — is internal TWG working material and is not published on the public docket, but is part of the TWG’s evidentiary record and subject to disclosure to congressional oversight committees upon request. (vi) Internal Transcription: the TWG Administrative Coordinator shall arrange for a verbatim transcript of each expert presentation, including the presentation itself and any TWG member questions and responses. The transcript shall be maintained in the TWG’s internal document repository, accessible to all TWG members and to the FAC Legislative Drafting Staff upon request. Internal transcripts are not published but shall be transmitted to the FAC alongside the relevant monthly work product submission or final domain recommendations as supporting documentation.

(P) Classified Information and Clearance Requirements.— Certain TWGs will necessarily receive classified information in the course of their investigatory mandate, including classified assessments of AI capability, classified export control determinations, classified threat assessments, and classified evaluations of autonomous weapons systems. The following provisions govern the handling of classified information by TWGs

(i) Clearance as Appointment Condition for Designated Slots: for each TWG slot designated as requiring access to classified information in subsections (e)(3) through (e)(13), clearance eligibility shall be a condition of appointment. The relevant appointing authority shall, before making an appointment to a designated slot, verify that the prospective appointee is eligible for the required clearance level and initiate the clearance process simultaneously with the appointment. A person who is ineligible for the required clearance level may not be appointed to a designated slot. The designation of which slots within each TWG require clearance eligibility shall be specified by the Director of AI Investigation in consultation with the relevant seed members, not later than Day 50, and published in an unclassified summary on the public portal. (ii) Non-Clearable Members: a TWG member who does not hold or cannot obtain the clearance required for a particular classified session shall be excluded from that session only. Exclusion from a classified session does not affect the member's standing, voting rights, or obligations with respect to all other TWG activities. The TWG Administrative Coordinator shall maintain a record of which members attended each classified session, and the TWG Chair shall ensure that classified information disclosed in a classified session is not discussed in any session from which non-cleared members are excluded without their knowledge. The TWG shall structure its work to minimize the number of classified sessions required and to maximize the participation of all members in unclassified work. (iii) Classified Session Procedures: classified sessions of a TWG shall be conducted in a facility approved for classified discussions at the relevant classification level, arranged by the TWG Administrative Coordinator in coordination with the relevant security authority. Classified sessions shall not be transcribed by the Administrative Coordinator; instead, a classified summary shall be prepared by a cleared TWG member designated by the TWG Chair, maintained in a classified document repository separate from the TWG's general document repository, and accessible only to cleared members of that TWG and to cleared FAC members. The public log entry for a classified session shall note only the date, the TWGs involved, and that a classified session was held; no subject matter description shall be included in the public log entry for a classified session. (iv) Classified Annex to Monthly Work Product Submissions: where a TWG's monthly work product submission includes findings, draft recommendations, or evidence derived from classified sources, the TWG shall produce a classified annex to that month's submission. The unclassified submission shall be published on the public docket as required by subsection (e)(2)(I). The classified annex shall be transmitted separately to cleared FAC members only, through secure channels established by the Technical Systems Manager in coordination with the relevant security authority, and shall not be published on the public docket. (v) Classified Report to Congressional Oversight Committees: the FAC shall, not later than 30 days after the FAC backstop transmission deadline established under section 8(f), transmit a classified supplemental report to the Committee on Armed Services and the Select Committee on Intelligence of each chamber, summarizing all classified information considered by any TWG that bears on the legislative recommendations transmitted to Congress, and identifying any classified findings that Congress should consider in enacting Phase II legislation. The classified supplemental report shall be prepared by the FAC Legislative Drafting Staff in coordination with the relevant cleared TWG members and shall be reviewed by the Director of National Intelligence before transmission.

(e)(3) TWG 1 — Intellectual Property and Creator Rights.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(18) (Intellectual Property, Copyright, and AI Training Data).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 1 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the harms to rights holders from unauthorized use of copyrighted works in AI training data; the scale and nature of existing AI training data practices; the gaps in current federal law under 17 U.S.C. §§ 101–1205; the limitations of existing fair use doctrine as applied to AI training; and the populations most affected including individual creators, small publishers, and working artists. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: the application of the fair use doctrine under 17 U.S.C. § 107 to AI training data; the feasibility and design of a statutory opt-out registry for rights holders; the feasibility and design of a statutory compulsory licensing framework modeled on 17 U.S.C. § 115; the adequacy of voluntary and collective licensing markets; training data provenance and disclosure standards; the constitutional authority of Congress under Article I, Section 2008, Clause 8 for each proposed intervention; and the option of no regulation or repeal of provisions determined to be counterproductive. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: the effects of each approach on innovation, competition, creator livelihoods, and public access to AI systems. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language for the recommended approach or a formal no-regulation finding with supporting rationale.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Senior Copyright Office attorney with AI training data and fair use expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career USPTO official with AI-generated works and patent eligibility expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career Copyright Royalty Board official with compulsory licensing and royalty rate expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career Library of Congress digital preservation official with AI digitization expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 1 shall include the following members appointed through the Stage Two nomination and selection process:

(1) AI Technical Expert (mandatory): a machine learning researcher or engineer with direct experience building or evaluating large language models or generative image, audio, or video models, with peer-reviewed work on training data memorization, model extraction, verbatim reproduction, or the intersection of copyright and machine learning; must not have an active financial interest in pending AI copyright litigation.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of harms to creators from AI-generated content, including market displacement of human creative work, unauthorized reproduction of copyrighted material by deployed models, or the economics of AI substitution in creative industries.

(3) Technical Memorization and Retrieval Expert: an engineer or researcher with expertise in retrieval-augmented generation, model memorization, and the technical parameters of verbatim reproduction in deployed models.

(4) Former IP Litigation Attorney: a former attorney who has concluded significant AI training data cases; currently active litigants are excluded per subsection (e)(1)(K).

(5) Copyright Markets Economist: an economist specializing in copyright markets, licensing structures, and the economics of intellectual property.

(6) Publisher Representative: a representative of book, news, or magazine publishers with direct experience in AI licensing negotiations or copyright enforcement.

(7) Visual Creator Representative: a representative of visual artists, illustrators, or photographers with direct experience in AI image generation disputes.

(8) Music Industry Representative: a representative of recording artists, composers, or music publishers with direct experience in AI-generated music or voice cloning disputes.

(9) Open-Source Developer: an open-source or open-weight software developer with expertise in the governance implications of publicly available AI models and training datasets.

(10) Constitutional IP Scholar: an academic with demonstrated expertise in the constitutional authority of Congress under Article I, Section 2008, Clause 8 and its application to AI training data legislation.

(11) Collective Licensing Practitioner: a practitioner with direct operational experience administering a collective licensing organization or performing rights organization.

(e)(4) TWG 2 — Electoral Integrity and Democratic Resilience.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(17) (Electoral Integrity and AI-Generated Disinformation).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 2 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the harms to democratic processes from AI-generated synthetic media, including documented incidents of electoral deepfake use; the scale of AI-generated disinformation; gaps in current federal law under the Federal Election Campaign Act and related statutes; and the affected populations including candidates, election workers, and voters. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: mandatory disclosure standards for AI-generated content in federal election communications; the constitutional pathway for federal regulation of electoral deepfakes consistent with First Amendment requirements established in Buckley v. Valeo, Citizens United, and United States v. Alvarez; mandatory FEC rulemaking parameters; C2PA content provenance standards; AI-powered voter suppression and microtargeting; foreign state actor AI interference; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing law. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: the First Amendment implications of each approach; enforcement feasibility; effects on political speech and satire. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or a formal no-regulation finding.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career FEC attorney or senior FEC enforcement official. (ii) Senate Minority: Career DOJ Election Crimes Branch official. (iii) Speaker: Career Election Assistance Commission official with election security expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career CISA election security official.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 2 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience building or evaluating synthetic media systems — diffusion models, voice synthesis, or video generation — with demonstrated technical understanding of what makes synthetic media detectable or undetectable and what disclosure mechanisms are technically enforceable at scale.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with peer-reviewed empirical documentation of AI-generated electoral disinformation campaigns or recommendation algorithm amplification of synthetic political content.

(3) Recommendation Algorithm Researcher: a researcher with technical understanding of how social media recommendation systems amplify synthetic content at scale.

(4) Former Senior Election Official: a former Secretary of State or former senior state election official; TWG 2 shall maintain strict partisan balance such that no more than one former election official has publicly identified affiliation with any single political party; currently serving election officials are excluded.

(5) First Amendment Scholar: an election law scholar with expertise in the First Amendment as applied to electoral speech and the constitutional parameters of federal regulation of political communications.

(6) Platform Trust and Safety Practitioner: a former or current platform trust and safety practitioner with direct election-specific experience.

(7) Foreign Interference Expert: a national security expert specializing in foreign state actor AI interference in elections.

(8) Disinformation Researcher: a political scientist or communications scholar specializing in AI-generated disinformation, with peer-reviewed empirical work.

(9) Election Integrity Civil Society Representative: a representative of a nonpartisan civil society organization specializing in election integrity or voting rights.

(10) Former Local Election Administrator: a former state or local election administrator; currently serving administrators are excluded.

(11) Content Provenance Technical Expert: a technical expert in cryptographic content provenance and authentication standards including C2PA or equivalent.

(e)(5) TWG 3 — Compute Infrastructure and Export Controls.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(13) (Compute Export Controls and Semiconductor Governance).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 3 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the harms from uncontrolled diffusion of advanced computing hardware; the current state of export control frameworks; gaps in existing law under the Export Control Reform Act of 2018; and the geopolitical implications of compute access asymmetries. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: the design of a successor framework to the rescinded AI Diffusion Framework; Know-Your-Customer requirements for compute providers; reporting obligations for training runs exceeding defined thresholds; governance of open-weight model exports; international allied coordination on compute controls; technical feasibility of compute monitoring and verification; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing BIS authority. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on United States AI competitiveness; risks of unilateral versus multilateral controls; enforcement feasibility. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or a formal no-regulation finding.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career BIS official with advanced technology export control expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career NSA or DARPA official with compute and semiconductor expertise (classified track). (iii) Speaker: Career Commerce NTIA official with semiconductor and infrastructure expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career CISA official with critical infrastructure and data center expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 3 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience designing or operating large-scale AI training infrastructure, with demonstrated technical understanding of what compute thresholds mean for model capability and what monitoring mechanisms are technically feasible.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with documented analysis of compute concentration risks, AI hardware market dynamics, or geopolitical risks from semiconductor supply chain dependencies.

(3) Frontier Hardware Expert: a hardware or systems engineer with expertise in frontier AI chip specifications, interconnect architecture, and data center design, with technical understanding of how export control thresholds interact with chip capabilities and potential circumvention methods.

(4) Semiconductor Industry Policy Specialist: a semiconductor industry engineer or policy specialist with direct experience in chip design, manufacturing, or supply chain governance.

(5) Cloud Infrastructure Representative: a cloud computing infrastructure representative with direct experience operating hyperscale data centers and large-scale AI training workloads.

(6) Arms Control Verification Expert: an arms control verification specialist with technical expertise in monitoring regime design and inspection protocols.

(7) Allied Nation Contributing Expert: a representative of an allied nation — the United Kingdom, Japan, the European Union, or South Korea — with expertise in compute governance and export control coordination; subject to the allied nation contributing expert carve-out under subsection (e)(1)(L)(i).

(8) Technology Competition Researcher: a national security researcher specializing in technology competition between the United States and the People's Republic of China.

(9) Compute Governance Academic: an academic or independent researcher specializing in compute governance, chip policy, or the political economy of AI infrastructure.

(10) Export Control Attorney: an international trade attorney with active export control practice including direct experience with BIS licensing and dual-use technology controls.

(11) Independent Chip Policy Researcher: an independent researcher with documented work on chip policy, compute thresholds, and the relationship between hardware access and AI capability development.

(e)(6) TWG 4 — Financial Markets and Antitrust.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(16) (Financial Markets, Algorithmic Trading, and AI-Driven Market Concentration).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 4 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: harms from AI-driven market concentration in frontier model development and compute infrastructure; algorithmic pricing coordination; systemic risk from AI-driven trading; disparate impact from AI financial services decisions; and the adequacy of existing antitrust and financial regulatory frameworks. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: antitrust remedies for AI-specific competitive dynamics; systemic risk regulations for AI trading; algorithmic pricing prohibitions; disparate impact standards for AI financial decisions; AI financial advice fiduciary obligations; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing FTC, SEC, CFTC, and CFPB authority. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on financial market efficiency, innovation, and consumer protection. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or a formal no-regulation finding.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career SEC economist or enforcement official with algorithmic trading and market structure expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career FTC economist or attorney with digital markets and AI competition expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career CFTC official with algorithmic markets and systemic risk expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career CFPB official with AI financial services and disparate impact expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 4 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience building or auditing AI systems used in financial markets — including RL trading agents, credit scoring algorithms, or fraud detection systems — with demonstrated understanding of how these systems make decisions and their failure modes under market stress.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of harms from AI in financial markets — algorithmic price coordination, disparate impact in AI-driven lending, or systemic risk from correlated AI trading strategies.

(3) Market Microstructure Expert: a quantitative finance or former high-frequency trading practitioner with technical understanding of how AI trading systems interact at market microstructure level.

(4) Digital Markets Antitrust Economist: an antitrust economist specializing in digital markets and market concentration.

(5) Systemic Risk Specialist: a financial markets systemic risk specialist with expertise in correlated failure modes in AI-driven financial systems.

(6) Consumer Finance Advocate: a consumer finance advocate with AI decision-making expertise, including direct experience representing consumers affected by AI-driven credit or insurance decisions.

(7) Disparate Impact Attorney: a civil rights attorney specializing in disparate impact in financial services with direct litigation or investigation experience.

(8) Financial Technology Representative: a financial technology company representative with direct experience developing or deploying AI financial services products.

(9) Former Fund Manager: a formerly serving institutional investor or recently retired quantitative fund manager; currently serving fund managers are excluded due to incompatible fiduciary duties.

(10) Antitrust Practitioner: a former DOJ Antitrust Division or former FTC competition attorney with direct technology sector experience.

(11) Financial Law Scholar: a securities, derivatives, or banking law scholar with expertise in financial market regulation and the application of existing frameworks to AI-driven market participants.

(e)(7) TWG 5 — AI Safety and Post-AGI Governance.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(12) (Post-AGI and Transformative AI Governance); the safety dimension of section 6(b)(15) (Open-Weight AI Models); and the dangerous capability evaluation content of section 6(b)(2) (Transparency, Evaluation, and Pre-Deployment Certification).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 5 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the probability, timeline, and precursor indicators of a Transformative AI Capability Event; the current state of dangerous capability evaluation methodology; gaps in existing evaluation standards; and the populations and institutions most at risk from transformative AI capabilities. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: the Pre-Determined Crisis Governance Framework required by section 6(b)(12)(B); dangerous capability evaluation standards for CBRN uplift, cyberoffense, deception, autonomous replication, and persuasion risk; pre-deployment safety certification regime design; the capability threshold at which open-weight model releases require mandatory pre-release approval; interpretability research implications for governance; deceptive alignment and its governance implications; the shelf-ready emergency legislative package required by section 18(c)(3); and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing authorities. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on AI innovation and United States competitiveness; risks of over-specification versus under-specification of capability thresholds. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language including the shelf-ready emergency package, or formal no-regulation findings with supporting rationale.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career DARPA official with AI safety or autonomous systems research expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career intelligence community official with AI capability assessment expertise (classified track) — appointment subject to mandatory post-employment restrictions review and ethics clearance before service begins. (iii) Speaker: Career NIST official with AI evaluation standards expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career DOE national laboratory official with AI safety research expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members, TWG 5 requires the following mandatory and additional members reflecting the technical depth of its mandate:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Dangerous Capability Evaluation (mandatory): a frontier AI researcher with direct experience conducting dangerous capability evaluations — specifically for CBRN uplift, autonomous replication, cyberoffense, or deceptive alignment — with peer-reviewed or government-commissioned evaluation work and demonstrated familiarity with the methodologies of METR, UK AISI, or equivalent evaluation bodies.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of AI capability progression, emergent capabilities, or AI systems exhibiting unexpected or dangerous behaviors, with peer-reviewed work on capability elicitation, scaling laws, or documented specification gaming or reward hacking.

(3) Interpretability Researcher: a researcher with direct experience developing or applying mechanistic interpretability tools to understand what AI systems are internally representing and computing.

(4) Alignment Researcher: a researcher working directly on the technical problem of aligning AI systems with human values and intentions — including RLHF, constitutional AI, debate, or scalable oversight — with demonstrated understanding of what current alignment methods can and cannot guarantee.

(5) CBRN Specialist: a biosecurity, chemical weapons, radiological, or nuclear security specialist with expertise in AI-enabled uplift assessment.

(6) Cyberoffense Expert: a cyberoffense or critical infrastructure security expert with expertise in AI-enabled attack capabilities.

(7) International AI Governance Scholar: a scholar or policy expert specializing in international AI governance frameworks.

(8) Arms Control Verification Specialist: an arms control verification expert with technical expertise in monitoring and inspection methodology applicable to AI capability thresholds.

(9) Existential Risk Philosopher or Ethicist: a philosopher or ethicist specializing in existential risk and transformative technology governance.

(10) Former Senior Classified AI Capability Assessor: a former senior official with classified AI capability assessment experience — subject to mandatory post-employment restrictions review; appointment contingent on ethics clearance confirming no ongoing obligations conflicting with TWG service.

(e)(8) TWG 6 — Children, Youth, and Vulnerable Populations.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(5) (AI and Mental Health; Harms to Vulnerable Populations); section 6(b)(6) (AI Companion Systems and Synthetic Intimacy); section 6(b)(7) (Recommendation Algorithms and Algorithmic Amplification); section 6(b)(8) (AI-Generated Child Sexual Abuse Material); and the child-specific interim protective measures of sections 15(c)(4) through 15(c)(9).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 6 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the documented harms to minors and vulnerable populations from AI companion systems, recommendation algorithms, AI-generated CSAM, and AI mental health tools; affected populations including minors across age tiers, children with mental health vulnerabilities, and families; and gaps in existing law under COPPA, CIPA, and related statutes. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: clinical standards for AI mental health tools; psychological harm measurement standards; AI companion system design prohibitions and required safety features for each age tier; algorithmic recommendation default standards for minor users; AI-generated CSAM detection and reporting standards; age verification and age assurance technical standards; mandatory crisis intervention protocols; duty of care standards; non-consensual intimate imagery prohibition standards; disclosure standards for adult users of attachment-maximizing AI systems; and the option of no regulation or repeal of provisions determined to be counterproductive. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on First Amendment speech, innovation, and parental rights; enforcement feasibility and technical implementability of each approach. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or formal no-regulation findings.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career FTC official with COPPA enforcement and child privacy expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career HHS official with child welfare and adolescent mental health expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career DOJ official from ICAC Task Force or FBI Crimes Against Children unit. (iv) House Minority: Career Department of Education official with student safety and technology expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— Notwithstanding the general size requirement of subsection (e)(2), TWG 6 shall be composed of not fewer than 15 members reflecting the breadth of its multi-domain mandate. In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 6 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Recommendation and Companion Systems (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience building or auditing recommendation algorithms, engagement optimization systems, or AI companion systems, with demonstrated understanding of how attachment-maximizing design features are implemented at the code level and what technical interventions are feasible and auditable.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with peer-reviewed empirical documentation of harms to minors from AI systems — algorithmic amplification of self-harm content, AI companion system psychological effects on adolescents, eating disorder or suicide content recommendation, or AI-generated CSAM.

(3) Former Platform AI Systems Engineer: a former platform engineer or product designer with direct experience designing engagement systems, recommendation algorithms, or AI companion features, with ability to assess the technical feasibility of safe-by-design requirements.

(4) Licensed Child Psychiatrist: a licensed child and adolescent psychiatrist with clinical expertise in technology-related mental health disorders.

(5) Licensed Child Psychologist: a licensed child psychologist specializing in technology use and adolescent development.

(6) Pediatric Neuroscientist: a pediatric neuroscientist or adolescent brain development researcher with expertise in the neurobiological mechanisms by which digital engagement systems affect developing brains.

(7) Eating Disorder Treatment Clinician: a licensed clinician with direct adolescent eating disorder treatment experience and expertise in algorithmic content amplification.

(8) Suicide Prevention Specialist: a licensed clinician or public health researcher specializing in adolescent suicide prevention and the relationship between social media and AI systems and suicide ideation.

(9) Law Enforcement Child Safety Specialist: a current or former law enforcement official with ICAC Task Force or FBI Crimes Against Children unit experience, with ability to assess whether proposed frameworks are operationally effective.

(10) Child Safety Organization Representative: a representative from the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children, Thorn, or an equivalent child sexual abuse prevention organization.

(11) Platform Trust and Safety Practitioner: a former or current platform trust and safety practitioner with direct minor-safety specialization.

(12) Parent Advocate: a representative of families of minors who have suffered documented harm from AI companion systems, recommendation algorithms, or AI-generated CSAM.

(13) Member Under 25: a member under the age of 25 at the time of appointment, representing the perspective of the affected population; subject to the flexible participation carve-out under subsection (e)(1)(L)(iii); full voting member.

(14) Child Welfare Policy Expert: a child welfare policy expert with experience designing or evaluating federal child safety regulatory programs.

(15) Age Verification Technical Expert: a technical expert in age verification and age assurance systems, with direct experience designing or evaluating technical mechanisms for verifying user age in online platforms.

(e)(9) TWG 7 — Workforce, Labor, and Economic Transition.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(1) (Workforce Displacement, Labor Market Disruption, and Economic Transition).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 7 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the scale, pace, and distributional effects of AI-driven workforce displacement; affected populations including low-wage workers, workers without college degrees, workers in AI-exposed industries, and independent workers; and gaps in existing law under the NLRA, WARN Act, Trade Adjustment Assistance, and related worker protection statutes. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: transition assistance, retraining, and income support frameworks; taxation of AI-generated productivity gains; labor rights in AI-managed workplaces; the adequacy of existing worker protection statutes; mechanisms to ensure AI economic gains are broadly shared; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing authorities. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on AI deployment incentives, business investment, and overall economic growth; distributional effects of each approach. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or formal no-regulation findings.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career DOL economist with automation and labor displacement expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career Treasury or CEA economist with AI macroeconomic impact expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career ETA official with worker transition program expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career NLRB or EEOC official with AI workplace and labor rights expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 7 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Workplace Automation (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience building or evaluating AI systems used in workplace automation — robotic process automation, agentic task execution, AI coding assistants, or AI-driven logistics — with demonstrated understanding of which tasks are automatable at current capability levels and what the near-term automation frontier looks like.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with peer-reviewed empirical documentation of workforce displacement from AI deployment — sector-level employment effects, wage suppression, task substitution patterns, or differential impacts by income, education, race, or geography.

(3) Agentic AI Systems Researcher: a researcher with direct expertise in agentic AI systems and their capacity for autonomous task completion across knowledge work domains.

(4) Labor Displacement Economist: a labor economist specializing in technological displacement, with peer-reviewed work on automation and employment effects.

(5) Organized Labor Representative: a representative of organized labor with direct experience representing workers in AI-exposed industries.

(6) Worker Retraining Specialist: a workforce development specialist with direct experience designing or operating worker retraining or community college workforce programs.

(7) Independent Worker Representative: a representative of independent workers, freelancers, or gig economy workers.

(8) Tax Policy Economist: a tax policy economist with expertise in designing taxes on AI-generated productivity gains or equivalent fiscal distribution mechanisms.

(9) Community Development Specialist: a community development specialist with direct experience in deindustrialized or economically displaced regions.

(10) Large Employer Practitioner: a human resources or future of work practitioner from a large employer with direct experience managing AI-driven workforce transitions.

(11) Social Safety Net Expert: a social safety net or income support policy expert with expertise in programs for workers displaced by technological change.

(12) AI Workforce Equity Researcher: a researcher with peer-reviewed empirical documentation of the differential impacts of automation, algorithmic management, or AI-driven hiring and employment decisions on workers by race, ethnicity, gender, disability status, age, or immigration status, including experience with civil rights frameworks applicable to AI-driven employment practices under Title VII, the ADA, the ADEA, or equivalent statutes.

(13) Labor Data Scientist: an economist or statistician with direct data science skills — including fluency in large administrative dataset analysis, machine learning applications in labor economics, and quantitative methods for measuring task-level and occupation-level automation exposure; preferred: documented applied work producing original empirical estimates of AI or automation impact on employment, wages, or workforce composition.

(e)(10) TWG 8 — Environmental, Energy, and Community Impact.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(10) (Environmental and Community Impacts of AI Data Centers).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 8 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: documented environmental and community harms from AI data center development including water consumption, grid stress, ratepayer cost impacts, air quality effects, and environmental justice disparities; affected communities including those in proximity to large data center clusters; and gaps in existing law under the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, NEPA, and FERC statutes. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: environmental impact assessment standards; water consumption disclosure and limitation standards; electricity demand and grid reliability frameworks; ratepayer cost allocation mechanisms; carbon footprint measurement and disclosure; renewable energy procurement requirements; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing EPA, FERC, and DOE authority. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on AI infrastructure development, energy costs, and community economic development. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or formal no-regulation findings.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career EPA official with data center environmental assessment expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career FERC official with grid interconnection and electricity demand expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career DOE official with data center energy efficiency expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career EJ official with community health and disparate impact expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 8 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Data Center Infrastructure (mandatory): a data center infrastructure engineer or researcher with direct experience designing, operating, or auditing large-scale AI computing facilities, with demonstrated technical understanding of GPU thermal management, cooling system architecture, PUE, WUE, and actual energy and water consumption profiles of frontier AI training and inference at scale.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of environmental and community harms from AI data center development — measured water consumption, grid stress effects, ratepayer cost impacts, air quality effects, or environmental justice impacts on host communities.

(3) Grid Systems Engineer: a grid systems engineer or electricity market researcher with technical understanding of how large data center loads interact with grid reliability, renewable energy procurement, and electricity pricing.

(4) Environmental Scientist: an environmental scientist specializing in water consumption and AI data center impacts.

(5) Environmental Justice Advocate: a representative of or advocate for a community near a large AI data center cluster with direct experience documenting environmental justice impacts.

(6) Renewable Energy Developer: a renewable energy developer or engineer with direct experience in data center siting and power purchase agreements.

(7) Community Health Researcher: a public health researcher specializing in air quality and community health impacts of industrial facilities.

(8) State Utility Regulator or Ratepayer Advocate: a state utility regulator or ratepayer advocate with direct experience in electricity rate proceedings affected by large industrial loads.

(9) Data Center Sustainability Expert: a data center operator or sustainability officer with direct experience implementing energy efficiency, water conservation, and renewable procurement programs.

(10) Climate Scientist: a climate scientist or carbon footprint researcher with AI-specific expertise in data center carbon accounting.

(11) Former Local Government Official: a former municipal or county official, or a currently serving appointed non-elected official, from a data center host community; currently serving elected local officials are excluded due to statutory duties incompatible with full-time service.

(e)(11) TWG 9 — Liability, Accountability, Legal Frameworks, and Constitutional Dimensions.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(3) (Liability, Accountability, Legal Frameworks, and Constitutional Dimensions of AI Governance).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 9 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: documented harms caused by deployed AI systems including wrongful benefit denials, biased hiring and credit decisions, medical misdiagnosis, and wrongful arrests; affected populations including those subject to AI-driven decisions in high-stakes domains; and gaps in existing tort law, product liability doctrine, and civil rights statutes; the question of AI legal personhood and standing — including whether AI systems may hold intellectual property, bear liability, or have legal interests cognizable by courts or Congress; and the constitutional landscape governing federal AI regulation, including First Amendment constraints on AI content mandates, disclosure requirements, compelled speech, and algorithmic transparency obligations across all domains investigated by this title’s Technical Working Groups. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: product liability frameworks for AI systems; strict liability versus negligence standards; private rights of action; algorithmic discrimination and civil rights enforcement; AI evidence in legal proceedings; corporate governance and board-level accountability; international harmonization of AI liability standards; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing common law and statutory frameworks. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on AI innovation incentives, insurance markets, and access to justice. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or formal no-regulation findings.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career DOJ Civil Division attorney with technology liability and administrative law expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career FTC attorney with consumer protection and AI enforcement expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career EEOC attorney with algorithmic discrimination and civil rights enforcement expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career ACUS or OLC official with regulatory design and administrative procedure expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 9 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Failure Modes and Auditing (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience auditing AI systems for failure modes — distribution shift failures, hallucination patterns, adversarial vulnerabilities, and output unreliability — with demonstrated understanding of how AI failures differ technically from conventional product failures.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of concrete harms caused by deployed AI systems — wrongful denials of benefits, biased decisions, medical misdiagnosis, or wrongful arrests from facial recognition.

(3) AI Deployment Lifecycle Expert: a software engineering or systems reliability expert with direct understanding of AI system deployment lifecycles — versioning, updates, fine-tuning, and how production AI systems evolve after initial deployment.

(4) Products Liability Scholar: a products liability or tort law scholar with demonstrated expertise in AI and autonomous systems liability.

(5) Administrative Law Scholar: an administrative law scholar with expertise in rulemaking design, enforcement architecture, and administrative procedure implications of AI regulation.

(6) Algorithmic Discrimination Attorney: a civil rights attorney specializing in algorithmic discrimination with direct litigation or investigation experience.

(7) AI Risk Actuary: an insurance or actuarial professional with direct experience quantifying AI risk and developing AI-specific insurance products.

(8) Healthcare AI Liability Expert: a healthcare liability attorney or medical professional with expertise in AI diagnostic liability and the standard of care for AI-assisted medical decisions.

(9) Criminal Law Scholar: a criminal law scholar with expertise in AI evidence, deepfakes in criminal proceedings, and AI-assisted crime.

(10) Corporate Governance Attorney: a corporate governance attorney with direct experience advising boards on AI accountability and director liability.

(11) International Comparative Law Scholar: a comparative law scholar with expertise in international AI liability frameworks including the EU AI Act.

(12) First Amendment Scholar: a constitutional law scholar with demonstrated expertise in First Amendment doctrine as applied to compelled speech, disclosure mandates, content moderation, and government regulation of expressive technologies, with peer-reviewed or equivalent published work on the constitutional parameters of regulating algorithmic or AI-generated content.

(13) Legal Philosopher — Personhood and Moral Status: a legal philosopher or jurisprudential scholar with peer-reviewed work on theories of legal personhood, moral status, or the extension of legal rights and capacities to non-human entities, with demonstrated engagement with AI-specific questions of agency, responsibility, and legal standing.

(e)(12) TWG 10 — AI Security, Critical Infrastructure, and Incident Response.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(4) (AI Security, Critical Infrastructure Protection, and Incident Response); and section 6(b)(9) (Agentic AI Systems).

(B) Mandate.— TWG 10 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: documented and potential AI-enabled attacks on critical infrastructure; AI-specific cybersecurity vulnerabilities in operational technology environments; AI-enabled social engineering and fraud; gaps in existing law under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, CISA authorities, and sector-specific cybersecurity statutes; the security and safety risks specific to agentic AI systems operating autonomously on behalf of users including risks arising from multi-agent orchestration; and affected populations including operators of critical infrastructure and users delegating real-world consequential actions to agentic AI systems; and the affirmative productivity potential of agentic AI systems, including documented cases where autonomous AI agents operating on behalf of knowledge workers in healthcare, legal, scientific research, financial analysis, software development, and other domains have materially increased individual or organizational productivity, the conditions under which that productivity is realized safely, and the governance gap between current regulatory frameworks and what would be needed to enable broad-based access to agentic AI productivity gains. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: AI-specific cybersecurity standards for critical infrastructure; mandatory AI incident reporting standards; AI system supply chain security requirements; sector-specific AI security requirements for energy, water, healthcare, and financial infrastructure; international coordination on AI security standards; hardware-level compute monitoring and tamper-proof verification systems; aviation-style AI incident reporting modeled on the NTSB; regulatory standards requiring agentic AI system providers to operate exclusively in the interest of the user with no undisclosed conflicts of interest; disclosure requirements, user-consent frameworks, and fiduciary-like obligations for AI agents taking actions with real-world consequences including financial transactions, legal commitments, communications, and healthcare decisions; technical safeguard standards for agentic AI systems including human-in-the-loop requirements calibrated to reversibility and consequence level, mandatory action logging and audit trail requirements, reversibility requirements for defined consequence categories, sandboxing and scope limitation requirements, and mandatory shutdown and override mechanisms operable by the user at any stage of an agentic workflow; governance of multi-agent orchestration systems including liability allocation across agent chains and emergent harmful behavior risks; regulatory safe harbor frameworks that would enable responsible agentic AI deployment in high-productivity domains — including healthcare, legal services, scientific research, and financial analysis — while maintaining user-protection standards; governance mechanisms that would ensure the productivity gains from agentic AI are broadly accessible rather than concentrated among large enterprises with the resources to develop proprietary agentic systems; and the option of no regulation or reliance on existing authorities. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects of each proposed intervention on AI deployment in critical sectors, agentic AI innovation, and international competitiveness; the tradeoffs between user-protection mandates and the flexibility required to realize agentic AI productivity gains at scale; the distributional effects of each approach on access to agentic AI tools across firm size, sector, and income level; and constitutional parameters for user-protection mandates on agentic systems. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language or formal no-regulation findings. TWG 10 shall coordinate with TWG 9 (Liability, Accountability, and Legal Frameworks) on liability allocation across agentic AI systems and multi-agent chains, and with TWG 5 (AI Safety and Post-AGI Governance) on autonomous AI system governance.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career CISA official with AI security and critical infrastructure protection expertise. (ii) Senate Minority: Career NSA or CYBERCOM official with offensive and defensive AI cyber expertise (classified track). (iii) Speaker: Career FBI official with AI-related cybercrime and threat assessment expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career DHS S&T official with AI resilience and incident response expertise.

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 10 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Adversarial ML (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience in adversarial machine learning — prompt injection attacks, model poisoning, adversarial examples, jailbreaking, and AI system manipulation — with demonstrated technical understanding of the attack surface of deployed AI systems and feasible technical defenses.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of AI-enabled cyberattacks, AI-assisted social engineering, or AI exploitation of critical infrastructure vulnerabilities.

(3) AI Red-Teamer: a professional AI red-teamer with direct experience conducting structured adversarial evaluations of deployed AI systems for weaponizable capabilities against critical infrastructure.

(4) Critical Infrastructure Security Expert: an expert in critical infrastructure sector security — energy, water, healthcare, or financial systems — with direct experience assessing AI-specific vulnerabilities in operational technology environments.

(5) Incident Response Practitioner: a practitioner with direct experience managing large-scale cybersecurity breach responses, with ability to design AI-specific incident response protocols.

(6) Telecommunications Security Expert: a telecommunications or internet infrastructure security expert with expertise in AI threats to network infrastructure.

(7) Healthcare Cybersecurity Official: a healthcare or hospital system cybersecurity official with direct experience addressing AI security risks in clinical environments.

(8) Financial Sector Cybersecurity Official: a financial sector cybersecurity official with direct experience addressing AI security risks in financial market infrastructure.

(9) State and Local Government Cybersecurity Official: a state or local government cybersecurity official with direct experience protecting government AI systems.

(10) AI Security Standards Expert: an AI security standards researcher or NIST AI Risk Management Framework practitioner with direct experience developing or implementing AI-specific security standards.

(11) Supply Chain Security Expert: a supply chain security expert with direct experience in AI hardware and software integrity — specifically the security of chip fabrication, firmware, and model weight supply chains.

(e)(13) TWG 11 — Autonomous Weapons, International Humanitarian Law, and Arms Control.—

(A) Domain.—Section 2006(b)(19) (Autonomous Weapons Systems: Governance, Standards, and International Frameworks); the definitions in sections 3(26) and 3(29); and the Red Line provisions of section 20(a)(1) as they apply to autonomous weapons systems.

(B) Mandate.— TWG 11 shall execute its mandate in the four phases of subsection (e)(2)(L) with respect to the following subject matter

(i) Domain Examination: the current state of autonomous weapons development and deployment; documented failures and risks of autonomous engagement systems; gaps in existing law and Department of Defense policy; and the implications of autonomous weapons for international humanitarian law and strategic stability. (ii) Intervention Evaluation: technical standards and criteria for the meaningful human oversight requirement; the boundary between lawful semi-autonomous systems and prohibited autonomous weapons; Department of Defense program compliance assessment methodology; the United States negotiating position for international LAWS agreements under the Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons; technical standards for tamper-proof authorization logging; Defensive Emergency Autonomy trigger threshold specifications; post-engagement reporting protocols; and the option of no additional regulation or reliance on existing DOD Directive 3000.09. (iii) Tradeoff Assessment: effects on United States military effectiveness and deterrence; implications for the international arms control regime; risks of unilateral restraint. (iv) Recommendation Development: specific draft statutory language including technical standards, or formal no-regulation findings. TWG 11 shall coordinate with the Department of Defense Inspector General for access to classified information through the classified track under section 5(e). TWG 11 shall be the lead TWG for joint sessions with TWG 5 on matters intersecting autonomous weapons governance and AI safety.

(C) Seed Members.—(i) Senate Majority: Career DOD official with autonomous systems acquisition or policy expertise, appointed in consultation with the Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. (ii) Senate Minority: Career State Department official with CCW or arms control negotiating expertise. (iii) Speaker: Career DARPA or defense research official with autonomous systems engineering expertise. (iv) House Minority: Career DIA official with autonomous weapons threat assessment expertise (classified track).

(D) Required Composition.— In addition to the 4 seed members and the mandatory AI expertise requirements of subsection (e)(2)(A), TWG 11 shall include:

(1) AI Technical Expert — Autonomous Targeting Systems (mandatory): a researcher or engineer with direct experience designing or evaluating autonomous targeting systems — sensor fusion architectures, object detection and classification systems, and engagement decision-making at millisecond timescales — with demonstrated technical understanding of what meaningful human oversight means at the decision timescales of autonomous weapons.

(2) Domain-Specific AI Harm Researcher (mandatory): a researcher with empirical documentation of autonomous weapons system failures, unintended engagement incidents, or reliability failures under adversarial or degraded conditions.

(3) Computer Vision and Sensor Fusion Engineer: a computer vision or sensor fusion engineer with direct expertise in how autonomous targeting systems identify and classify targets under real-world conditions including the failure modes under camouflage, electronic jamming, or adversarial manipulation.

(4) International Humanitarian Law Scholar: an international humanitarian law scholar specializing in the law of armed conflict and the application of IHL principles — distinction, proportionality, precaution — to autonomous weapons systems.

(5) Arms Control Verification Specialist: an arms control treaty specialist with direct verification and monitoring expertise applicable to autonomous weapons limitations.

(6) Recently Retired Military Officer: a recently retired military officer (within 5 years of separation from active duty) with direct operational experience with autonomous or semi-autonomous weapons systems; if the appointing authority determines that active-duty expertise is essential and unavailable from recently retired officers, an active-duty officer may be detailed under the carve-out of subsection (e)(1)(L)(ii).

(7) Defense Contractor Representative: a defense contractor representative with autonomous systems engineering expertise and direct experience in defense acquisition programs.

(8) Autonomous Weapons Civil Society Representative: a representative of a civil society organization specializing in autonomous weapons governance — the Campaign to Stop Killer Robots or equivalent.

(9) Former Arms Control Diplomat: a former State Department official or diplomat with direct CCW negotiating experience and expertise in multilateral arms control processes.

(10) State Responsibility Law Scholar: a legal scholar specializing in state responsibility, accountability for autonomous systems, and the application of international law to AI-enabled warfare.

(11) Allied Nation Contributing Expert: a representative of an allied nation with significant autonomous weapons development or governance activity — the United Kingdom, Australia, France, or Germany; subject to the allied nation contributing expert carve-out under subsection (e)(1)(L)(i).

(e)(16) Election of TWG Representatives to the Federal Advisory Committee.—

(e)(16)(A) Timing.—Each TWG shall elect one representative to the Federal Advisory Committee not later than 30 days after the TWG's first organizational meeting — that is, not later than Day 155. The election shall take place by majority vote of the TWG's full membership during a duly noticed TWG session.

(e)(16)(B) Eligibility.—Any current full member of the TWG is eligible to serve as the TWG's FAC representative. A member simultaneously serving as the TWG's Coordination Council primary representative is eligible and may hold both roles concurrently if the TWG determines that person is best suited to both functions. The allied nation contributing expert serving under subsection (e)(1)(L)(i) and the under-25 member serving under subsection (e)(1)(L)(iii) may not serve as FAC representative.

(e)(16)(C) Qualifications for FAC Service.— In electing their FAC representative, TWG members shall prioritize the member who

(i) has the deepest substantive command of the TWG's domain findings and the technical nuance of the legislative questions arising from that domain

(ii) is best positioned to translate the TWG's investigatory findings into binding statutory language — meaning the member has demonstrated ability to produce or critically evaluate legislative text, not merely policy analysis

(iii) can articulate the evidentiary basis and limitations of the TWG's findings to the full FAC across all 19 domains

(iv) can represent the TWG's collective conclusions in FAC deliberations — not as a delegate bound by TWG instruction but as the member whose expertise and judgment best embodies what the TWG has learned

(v) can commit to the additional time demands of FAC service alongside continued TWG membership.

(e)(16)(D) Distinction from Coordination Council Role.—The Coordination Council representative elected under subsection (e)(14) serves a coordination function distinct from the FAC representative's legislative drafting function. The Coordination Council representative must have sufficient command of the TWG's domain to harmonize definitions with other TWGs, resolve cross-domain jurisdictional questions, and share evidence effectively — but the primary qualification is coordination capacity and cross-domain fluency, not depth of technical expertise in any single domain. A member who excels at coordination may be the right choice for the Coordination Council role even if another member has deeper technical expertise; conversely, the most technically expert member may be the right FAC representative even if less suited to the coordination role. TWGs are encouraged to consider these distinct qualifications separately, while recognizing that a member possessing both qualities may appropriately hold both roles concurrently.

(e)(16)(E) Continued TWG Membership.—A TWG member elected to the FAC does not leave the TWG. They serve on both bodies simultaneously. Their TWG obligations — including participation in monthly work product submissions, weekly all-day sessions, cross-TWG coordination sessions, and ongoing investigatory work — continue in full. The FAC representative role is additive, not substitutive. The TWG shall not be depleted of a member's expertise by their election to the FAC. The Federal Advisory Committee Chair and the TWG Chair shall coordinate scheduling to minimize conflicts between FAC and TWG obligations for dual-role members.

(e)(16)(F) FAC Seat Activation.—A TWG representative's FAC seat activates upon election, not upon completion of the TWG's investigatory work. TWG representatives join the FAC as they are elected beginning approximately Day 155. The FAC therefore has a rolling constitution: the 7 appointed members are seated from Day 60; TWG representatives join on a rolling basis from Day 155 through Day 165 as all 11 elections complete; the FAC is fully constituted at 18 members approximately Day 165, at which point the FAC Chair election occurs and the 12-vote voting threshold for legislative provisions becomes operative.

(e)(16)(G) Legislative Drafting Support Access.—From the date a TWG representative joins the FAC, the FAC's Legislative Drafting Staff are available to that representative and to the TWG for legislative drafting assistance, constitutional analysis, and cross-domain drafting coordination. TWGs may also submit research requests to the Congressional Research Service through the formal channel managed by the Executive Director. Legislative drafting support is available to TWGs throughout the investigatory period upon request, not only after FAC election.

(e)(16)(H) Fallback.—If any TWG fails to elect an FAC representative within 30 days of its organizational meeting, the Executive Director of the Federal Advisory Committee shall designate an FAC representative from among that TWG's full membership within 7 days thereafter, selecting the member who best meets the qualifications of subparagraph (C).

(e)(16)(I) Recall and Replacement.—A TWG may recall its FAC representative by two-thirds vote of full TWG membership at any time, following the same recall procedures applicable to Coordination Council representatives under subsection (e)(14)(B)(iv). Upon recall, the TWG shall elect a replacement representative within 15 days. If the TWG fails to elect a replacement within 15 days, the Executive Director of the Federal Advisory Committee shall designate a replacement from among that TWG's full membership within 7 days thereafter.

(e)(14) TWG Coordination Council.—

(e)(14)(A) Establishment and Composition.—There is established a TWG Coordination Council (in this subsection, the ‘Coordination Council’) composed of one primary representative and one deputy representative elected from each of the 11 TWGs, for a total of 11 primary representatives and 11 deputy representatives, and the Epistemic Advisor appointed under subsection (b)(1)(G) as a standing non-voting participant. The Epistemic Advisor shall attend all Coordination Council sessions with full speaking rights and may not be excluded from any session by the Coordination Council presiding officer or any TWG representative. The Epistemic Advisor’s role in Coordination Council proceedings is to identify cross-TWG epistemic inconsistencies — including cases where two or more TWGs are drawing materially different inferences from the same underlying evidence, where the Coordination Council’s definitional or jurisdictional harmonization decisions appear driven by considerations other than evidentiary reasoning, or where the shared evidence repository reflects systematic gaps or imbalances in evidence-gathering across domains. The Epistemic Advisor may submit written observations on any Coordination Council matter to the presiding officer at any time; such observations shall be entered in the public Coordination Council record and transmitted to the relevant TWG Administrative Coordinators within 5 business days. The Coordination Council may hold meetings and conduct its functions with fewer than 11 primary representatives present during any period before all 11 TWGs have held their organizational meetings; a quorum of the Coordination Council shall consist of a majority of the primary representatives then elected. Coordination Council resolutions adopted before all 11 representatives are seated are binding but shall be made available for reconsideration within 10 days of a newly seated representative’s request, if the newly seated representative demonstrates that the resolution materially affects their TWG’s domain. The Coordination Council shall elect a presiding officer from among the primary TWG representatives who have been elected at the time of the Council’s first meeting by majority vote, not later than Day 144. The presiding officer serves for the duration of the investigatory period and may be recalled by two-thirds vote of primary representatives. The presiding officer shall chair Council meetings, coordinate scheduling of joint sessions, and transmit written resolutions to the Federal Advisory Committee. The Coordination Council shall be a standing body for the duration of the investigatory period and shall continue to meet until both of the following have occurred: the Federal Advisory Committee has completed its legislative transmission to Congress under subsection (f); and the National AI Council has assumed its full mandate. The Coordination Council shall remain operational through any overlap period between the FAC’s legislative transmission and the National AI Council’s full constitution, providing continuity of cross-domain coordination.

(e)(14)(B) Election of Representatives.—

(i) Timing.—Each TWG shall elect its primary representative and deputy representative to the Coordination Council not later than 14 days after the TWG's first organizational meeting — that is, not later than Day 139. The election shall take place during the TWG's normal working sessions. The election shall be conducted by separate majority vote of the full TWG membership and shall be the TWG's first order of business within its first 14 days of operation following the organizational meeting.

(ii) Role and Eligibility — Coordination Council Representative.—The Coordination Council representative serves a coordination function: harmonizing definitions across TWGs, resolving cross-domain jurisdictional questions, sharing evidence, and ensuring the 11 parallel investigations remain coherent and non-duplicative. In electing their Coordination Council representative, TWG members shall prioritize the member who: has sufficient command of the TWG's domain to represent its work accurately to other TWGs; possesses the temperament and communication skills to work constructively across domain boundaries; can identify when another TWG's work intersects with their own domain and flag it promptly; and can absorb and relay information across domains without losing the technical integrity of their own TWG's findings. Deep technical expertise in the TWG's domain is valuable but secondary to cross-domain fluency and coordination effectiveness. A member elected as Coordination Council representative may also serve as the TWG's FAC representative under subsection (b)(1); the two roles may be held concurrently by the same member if the TWG determines that person is best suited to both functions. Only current members of the TWG are eligible to serve as primary representative or deputy representative. No outside hire, staff member, contractor, or person who is not a duly appointed TWG member may serve in either capacity. A member who is elected as primary representative of one TWG may not simultaneously serve as deputy representative of any other TWG.

(iii) Term.—The primary representative and deputy representative each serve for the duration of the TWG's mandate unless earlier removed pursuant to the recall procedures of clause (iv).
