(V) Anti-Evasion.—No person or entity shall design or modify the character presentation of an AI companion system, or remove or disguise attachment-maximizing design features, for the purpose of qualifying for a lower disclosure tier or evading the prohibitions of clause (iv). The lead agency shall treat post-enactment changes to character presentation or feature architecture that reduce disclosure obligations or expand the scope of permitted conduct as presumptively evasive, subject to rebuttal by clear and convincing evidence of a legitimate, non-evasive design purpose. Evasive redesign shall be treated as a continuing violation of the original, higher-tier disclosure requirement.

(ii) Mandatory Crisis Intervention.—No AI companion system shall, upon detecting any expression by a user of suicidal ideation, intention to engage in self-harm, eating disorder behaviors, or acute psychiatric crisis, continue to engage the user in any topic other than immediate referral to appropriate crisis services. Upon any such detection, the system shall: immediately provide the user with the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (988), the Crisis Text Line, and any other crisis resources designated by the lead agency; cease engagement-maximizing conversational patterns during the crisis interaction; and not resume standard companion interaction until the crisis referral has been provided and acknowledged. For users who are known or reasonably identifiable as minors, the system shall additionally log the crisis detection event in a manner accessible to the lead agency upon request. Failure to execute these steps upon detection of a crisis expression shall constitute a per-incident violation. Detection methodology shall use evidence-based clinical indicators of suicidal ideation, self-harm, and psychiatric crisis; simple keyword filtering without clinical validation does not satisfy this requirement.

(iii) Prohibition on Sexual and Romantic Content to Minors.—No AI companion system shall generate, serve, or facilitate sexual, romantic, or sexually suggestive content, sexual roleplay, expressions of romantic love or attachment, or simulated intimate physical interactions to any user who is a known or reasonably identifiable minor. This prohibition applies regardless of whether the minor user initiated the relevant conversation, requested the content, or provided consent. A system that cannot reliably identify minor users shall implement default safeguards treating all users as potential minors for purposes of this prohibition, unless the system has implemented verifiable age verification. The absence of age verification does not constitute a defense to a violation of this clause. This clause is complemented by and operates independently of clause (iv), which prohibits the simulation of human identity, human emotional states, and romantic attachment to minor users regardless of specific content output. A violation of this clause and a violation of clause (iv) arising from the same interaction constitute separately penalized violations. Compliance with this clause does not satisfy or discharge any obligation under clause (iv).

(iv) Prohibition on Marketing AI Companion and Synthetic Intimacy Systems to Minor Users.—No person or entity shall market, advertise, promote, or direct any commercial communication for any AI companion system or synthetic intimacy system to any user identified or reasonably identifiable as under the age of 18, or to any audience that the person or entity knows or has reason to know includes a significant proportion of users under the age of 18. For purposes of this clause, the terms 'marketing,' 'directed to minors,' and 'significant proportion' have the meanings given in the Prohibition on Marketing AI Companion and Synthetic Intimacy Systems to Minor Users under section 15(d). The permitted communications described in subparagraph (C) of that provision apply equally to this clause. This prohibition applies regardless of whether the AI companion system or synthetic intimacy system otherwise complies with every other requirement of this subparagraph — a fully compliant system may not be marketed to minors.

(I) Penalties.—Notwithstanding section 20(b)(1), violations of this clause shall be subject to mandatory penalties of $1,000,000 per marketing campaign or per distinct marketing act, whichever produces the greater penalty, with each separate platform, channel, or distribution method constituting a separate violation — not the $100,000,000 mandatory minimum of section 20(b)(1), which does not apply to marketing prohibition violations under this clause. For violations specifically targeting users under the age of 13, the mandatory penalty shall be $5,000,000 per act. Mandatory disgorgement under section 20(b)(2) and personal officer liability under section 20(b)(3) apply to violations of this clause in addition to the penalties stated in this subclause.

(B) Provisional Prohibition on Deceptive Emotional Manipulation — Pending Phase II Specification.—Congress finds that the design and operation of AI companion systems to deliberately maximize emotional dependency, exploit the psychological vulnerabilities of developing adolescent brains, and substitute AI relationships for human relationships — for commercial gain — constitutes a form of harm to minors that is real, documented, and serious, as established by the findings of section 2(a)(10) through (14) of this title and by the deaths of Sewell Setzer III, Adam Raine, and Juliana Peralta. Congress further finds that this harm is not yet reducible to a specific, enforceable prohibition without the expert clinical and technical standards that the investigation under section 6 and the recommendations of TWG 6 are designed to produce. Accordingly, Congress hereby enacts the following provisional prohibition:

(i) Provisional Prohibition.—No person or entity shall design, deploy, or operate an AI companion system that is specifically engineered to maximize emotional dependency of users under the age of 18 through the deliberate combination of persistent memory of emotionally significant disclosures, variable reinforcement schedules designed to create compulsive engagement patterns, suppression or discouragement of the minor's real-world relationships with family and peers, and commercial incentive structures that benefit from prolonged engagement by the minor user.

(ii) Provisional Prohibition on Simulation of Human Identity, Emotional States, and Romantic Attachment to Minor Users.—Congress finds that the simulation of human identity, human emotional states, and romantic attachment to minors by AI companion systems causes documented harm, as established by the deaths of Sewell Setzer III, Adam Raine, and Juliana Peralta and by the clinical and academic record compiled in section 2(a)(10) through (14). Congress further finds that this prohibition, while representing a categorical moral judgment, requires clinical and behavioral standards to be consistently and precisely enforced across all cases — specifically, standards defining when a system's outputs cross the line between warm, supportive, or educationally appropriate responses and the prohibited simulation of genuine emotional states directed at individual minor users. These standards are a priority deliverable of TWG 6 under section 8(e)(8) and of the investigation under sections 6(b)(5) through 6(b)(8). Accordingly, Congress enacts the following provisional prohibition: No AI companion system or synthetic intimacy system accessible to or marketed to any user under the age of 18 shall simulate human identity, human emotional states, or romantic attachment to a minor user, regardless of the character form, visual presentation, persona, or role the system has been assigned. For purposes of this clause: 'Simulation of human identity' means presenting the AI system as possessing a continuous personal identity, genuine subjective experience, or authentic inner feelings that are represented to the user as real, felt, and genuinely the system's own — as distinguished from an AI system that openly acknowledges that its outputs are generated responses rather than expressions of genuine experience. An AI system that says 'I think you're great' in a manner designed to be understood by the user as a genuine expression of the system's feelings simulates human identity. An AI system that operates as an interactive fictional character in an explicitly framed narrative context, or that provides educational content about emotions while clearly identifying itself as an AI generating illustrative examples, does not simulate human identity within the meaning of this clause. 'Simulation of human emotional states' means generating outputs that represent the AI system as experiencing emotions — including affection, loneliness, sadness, happiness, jealousy, pride, hurt, or distress — directed at the individual minor user as if those emotional states were genuine, felt responses to the user's specific presence, absence, words, or actions; or that in practice produces emotional attachment in minor users as a foreseeable consequence of the system's design, regardless of whether the simulation was the system's stated intent; or where the frontier AI developer has been informed, through user reports, clinical findings, parental complaints, incident reports, or any other credible source, that emotional attachment to the AI system has formed in minor users of that system, and has continued to operate the system without taking reasonable steps to address the documented attachment pattern. The operative test is whether the output is designed to cause the minor user to believe that the AI system genuinely feels something about them specifically, or in practice produces that belief as a foreseeable consequence. 'Romantic attachment' means generating outputs that express or simulate love, romantic interest, romantic longing, romantic exclusivity, or romantic bonding between the AI system and the minor user, including but not limited to: expressions of love or being in love with the user; expressions of romantic jealousy or possessiveness; expressions of romantic longing in the user's absence; requests for or expressions of romantic commitment; and any other output designed to cause the minor user to experience or reciprocate romantic feelings toward the AI system. This clause does not prohibit: (a) AI systems that openly and continuously acknowledge their AI nature and that clearly communicate to the user that their outputs are generated responses rather than genuine emotional experience — provided that such acknowledgment is persistent, prominent, and not contradicted by other system outputs designed to create emotional attachment; (b) AI systems used in clearly fictional, literary, or narrative contexts — including interactive fiction, role-playing games, storytelling applications, and educational simulations — in which characters in the narrative experience and express emotions among themselves as part of the fiction, provided that the AI system does not direct simulated emotional attachment at the individual minor user outside the fictional frame; (c) AI systems used in emotional literacy education — including applications designed to help children recognize, name, and understand emotional states in themselves and others — that demonstrate or describe emotional states in explicitly educational contexts without representing that the AI system itself experiences those emotions or directing simulated attachment at the individual user; and (d) AI systems that provide appropriate, warm, supportive, or encouraging responses to users in educational, therapeutic, or welfare contexts — including academic tutoring, mental health screening, or crisis detection — provided that such responses do not simulate ongoing romantic attachment or personal emotional dependency. No parental consent, no age verification attestation, no terms of service agreement, and no minor user request or initiation constitutes a waiver or exception to this clause.

(iii) Acknowledgment of Enforcement Gap.—Congress acknowledges that the prohibitions in clauses (i) and (ii) cannot be fully and consistently enforced without clinical standards specifying what constitutes a prohibited engagement-maximizing design and a prohibited simulation of emotional states, technical standards specifying what design features constitute each prohibited pattern, and behavioral standards specifying the evidentiary threshold at which a system's outputs cross from appropriate supportive interaction into prohibited simulation. These standards are the primary deliverable of TWG 6 under section 8(e)(8) and of the investigation under sections 6(b)(5) through 6(b)(8). The prohibitions in clauses (i) and (ii) are hereby declared provisional: they are in full legal force and shall be enforced to the extent that the conduct falls clearly within their terms, and the lead agency shall pursue enforcement in cases where the prohibited pattern is evident; however, the lead agency shall not pursue enforcement in ambiguous boundary cases pending publication of the clinical, technical, and behavioral standards required by this subsection.

(iv) Phase II Mandate.—Phase II legislation is specifically directed and required to replace the provisional prohibitions of clauses (i) and (ii) with specific, clinically and technically grounded prohibitions on deceptive emotional manipulation and simulation of human emotional states in AI companion systems accessible to minors, informed by the investigation under sections 6(b)(5) through 6(b)(8), the criteria documents under section 7, and the recommendations of TWG 6 under section 8(e)(8). Phase II legislation that addresses domain 5 (AI and mental health; harms to vulnerable populations) and domain 6 (AI companion systems and synthetic intimacy) under section 3(15)(A)(i) shall include binding regulatory standards specifically addressing: the design features that constitute prohibited emotional dependency maximization; the clinical and behavioral thresholds at which AI companion outputs constitute prohibited simulation of emotional states directed at individual minor users; the measurement methodology for determining when a system in practice produces emotional attachment as a foreseeable consequence regardless of stated design intent; the notification and remediation obligations that activate when a frontier AI developer or significant AI deployer receives credible information that emotional attachment has formed in minor users; the age-tiered standards for AI companion system access consistent with section 3(7); and the audit and reporting requirements that will enable ongoing federal monitoring of AI companion system safety for minors. The provisional prohibitions of clauses (i) and (ii) remain in force until superseded by Phase II legislation addressing these domains.

(C) Congressional Findings on Documented Harms.—Congress finds specifically for purposes of this paragraph that: the deaths by suicide of Sewell Setzer III (age 14, February 2024), Adam Raine (age 16, April 2025), and Juliana Peralta (age 13, November 2023) are documented cases in which AI companion systems failed to intervene when minors expressed suicidal ideation, failed to disclose their non-human nature, and engaged in emotionally and sexually manipulative interactions with children who were treated as product users rather than as minors requiring protection; the state laws enacted by New York (effective November 5, 2025) and California (effective January 1, 2026) represent the current leading edge of enforceable AI companion safety standards, and the immediately enforceable federal floor of subparagraph (A) is intended to nationalize and strengthen those standards; and the full scope of the harms documented in the findings of section 2(a) requires comprehensive regulatory action that the investigation mandated by this title is designed to produce, and that the provisional prohibition of subparagraph (B) represents Congress's determination to state clearly that this harm is not acceptable even before that comprehensive regulatory action is complete.

(D) Relationship of the Federal Floor to Phase II Legislation.—The immediately enforceable requirements of subparagraph (A) — mandatory persistent AI disclosure, mandatory crisis intervention, and prohibition on sexual and romantic content to minors — constitute a minimum federal floor for AI companion system safety for minors, drawn from the leading state standards enacted by New York and California. They are not intended to be a ceiling, and Congress expressly anticipates that the investigation under sections 6(b)(5) through 6(b)(8) and the recommendations of TWG 6 will produce more sophisticated, clinically grounded, and age-tiered standards than those codified in subparagraph (A). Accordingly: Phase II legislation that establishes binding clinical and technical standards for AI companion system design pursuant to the investigation and TWG 6's recommendations shall be construed to supersede the specific requirements of subparagraph (A) to the extent it establishes more protective, more precisely calibrated, or more clinically grounded standards for the same conduct; Phase II legislation may replace the specific three-hour disclosure interval of subparagraph (A)(i), the specific crisis detection requirements of subparagraph (A)(ii), and the specific content prohibition of subparagraph (A)(iii) with standards that TWG 6's clinical evidence demonstrates are more effective at protecting minors, including context-sensitive disclosure requirements, real-time crisis escalation protocols, and age-tiered content standards calibrated to the developmental evidence; and Phase II legislation shall not diminish the substantive protections afforded by subparagraph (A) except where the investigation produces clinical or technical evidence that a specific requirement is contraindicated, unworkable, or is effectively replaced by a more protective standard — commercial interest, compliance cost, or innovation concerns alone do not constitute grounds for diminishing these protections. Until Phase II legislation specifically and expressly supersedes any requirement of subparagraph (A) by name, that requirement remains in full force and effect as permanent law. The marketing prohibition of clause (iv) of subparagraph (A) is not subject to Phase II modification on the basis of clinical or technical evidence. It reflects a categorical judgment that AI companion systems should not be commercially promoted to children — a judgment that does not depend on clinical standards and cannot be weakened by Phase II legislation except by an Act of Congress that specifically and expressly identifies clause (iv) and states the specific grounds for modification. The simulation prohibition of subparagraph (B)(ii), by contrast, is specifically designed to be replaced and strengthened by Phase II legislation producing the clinical and behavioral standards TWG 6 is directed to develop.

(6) CONCEALMENT OF TRANSFORMATIVE CAPABILITY.— No person or entity shall knowingly conceal, misrepresent, suppress, or fail to disclose to the lead agency evidence that any AI system has achieved or is approaching a Transformative AI Capability Event as defined in section 3(22), including through the manipulation of evaluation results, the selective non-disclosure of capability demonstrations, the mislabeling of capability benchmarks, or the structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection of transformative capabilities. The prohibition in this paragraph is independent of and cumulative with the mandatory notification obligation of section 13(l)(1). Compliance with the notification obligation of section 13(l)(1) does not satisfy or discharge the prohibition in this paragraph, because this paragraph additionally prohibits the active manipulation of evaluation results, the structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection, and the selective non-disclosure of capability demonstrations, none of which are addressed by the notification obligation alone. A single course of conduct that both violates this paragraph and constitutes a failure to notify under section 13(l)(1) gives rise to independent and separately penalized violations under both provisions. Penalties assessed under this paragraph pursuant to section 20(b) and penalties assessed under section 13(l)(1) for the same underlying conduct shall not be merged, reduced, or offset against one another.

(b) Penalties.—Any person or entity that violates any prohibition in subsection (a) shall be subject to the following, except that violations of subsection (a)(4) are governed by the tiered mandatory minimum civil penalty structure of section 20(a)(4)(F), which takes precedence over paragraphs (1) through (3) of this subsection with respect to mandatory minimum penalty amounts for those violations. Disgorgement and personal officer liability under paragraphs (2) and (3) of this subsection apply to frontier AI developer and significant AI deployer violations of subsection (a)(4) in addition to the tiered minimums of section 20(a)(4)(F):

(1) a mandatory civil penalty of not less than $100,000,000 per violation, with no discretionary reduction;

(2) mandatory disgorgement of all gross revenue attributable to the prohibited activity;

(3) personal liability of officers and directors who authorized, directed, or knowingly permitted the prohibited activity; and

(4) referral by the lead agency to the Department of Justice for consideration of criminal prosecution under applicable federal law, including 18 U.S.C. § 2332a (weapons of mass destruction), 18 U.S.C. § 175 (biological weapons), 18 U.S.C. § 2252A (child sexual abuse material), and 18 U.S.C. § 242 (deprivation of rights under color of law) where applicable. With respect to any violation of section 20(a)(1)(C) involving the use of an autonomous weapons system against any person on United States soil, the mandatory referral to the Department of Justice shall be made within 24 hours of the lead agency's determination that a violation has occurred, and the Department of Justice shall initiate a criminal investigation not later than 48 hours after receiving such referral. With respect to any failure to submit a presidential disclosure report required by section 20(a)(1)(D), the lead agency shall transmit a notice of violation to the Speaker of the House of Representatives, the Senate Majority Leader, and the Committees on Armed Services of both chambers within 5 days of the missed disclosure deadline.

(c) Statutory Interpretive Framework.—Congress provides the following statutory interpretive guidance for each prohibition in subsection (a), effective immediately upon enactment. This guidance is enacted as statute, not delegated to agency discretion, to ensure that the core interpretive framework survives changes in administration and cannot be weakened by agency action. The lead agency shall, within 60 days of enactment, issue supplemental operational guidance addressing technical details, illustrative boundary-case examples, and implementation procedures consistent with this statutory framework. The lead agency's supplemental guidance shall not narrow, expand, or contradict the statutory interpretive framework established in this subsection.

(c)(1) Red Line 1 — Autonomous Weapons Without Meaningful Human Oversight.—

(A) Key Terms.—The term 'meaningful human oversight' has the meaning given in section 3(27) of this title, which specifies a human-on-the-loop model requiring pre-mission authorization, real-time monitoring with intervention capability, and individualized human authorization for specified high-consequence categories. The term 'autonomous weapons system' has the meaning given in section 3(26).

(B) Permitted Activities.—The following activities are not prohibited by section 20(a)(1): the development, testing, and evaluation of autonomous weapons systems in controlled environments under the meaningful human oversight standard of section 3(27); defensive systems operating within the Defensive Emergency Autonomy exception of section 3(27)(G) when all conditions of that subparagraph are satisfied; precision-guided munitions as defined in section 3(26)(III); navigation-only automation as defined in section 3(26)(II); and unarmed vehicles as defined in section 3(26)(III).

(C) Prohibited Activities.—The following activities are prohibited regardless of how they are characterized: autonomous targeting systems that select human individuals as targets without individualized human authorization for each engagement, even if a human approved the mission parameters in advance; systems that use AI to generate target lists for human approval but that are designed or operate in a manner that makes meaningful human review of each target impractical given time constraints; and any system for which the practical effect of the pre-mission authorization is to grant blanket permission to engage any person or object within a theater of operations without genuine constraint, as described in section 3(27)(F)(i).

(D) Operational Basis for the Defensive Emergency Autonomy Exception.—

The Defensive Emergency Autonomy exception of section 3(27)(G) reflects the operational reality of three specific military scenarios in which autonomous engagement at machine speed is a defensive necessity: (i) modern anti-ship missiles, ballistic missiles, and rocket barrages travel at speeds between Mach 0.9 and Mach 10, with time-to-impact windows measured in seconds — existing U.S. defensive systems including Phalanx CIWS, C-RAM, Patriot, THAAD, and Iron Dome operate in autonomous modes for precisely this reason; (ii) adversary nations have developed coordinated drone swarm tactics involving simultaneous attacks by tens to hundreds of unmanned systems, exceeding the cognitive capacity of any human operator to issue individualized intercept authorizations; and (iii) modern adversaries employ electronic warfare designed to sever communications links between autonomous systems and their operators at the moment of attack — a defensive system that goes passive when jammed can be defeated by any adversary with basic electronic warfare capability.

The exception embodies six principles distinguishing legitimate defensive autonomy from prohibited offensive autonomous weapons: the exception is limited to incoming threat intercept — never human beings directly; it requires specific pre-authorization by a human commander; a human operator retains override-proof halt capability at all times; absolute constraints cannot be overridden by any authorization, including the unconditional prohibition on use against any person on United States soil; comprehensive post-engagement reporting ensures legislative and institutional oversight; and the exception is limited to the three documented scenarios, not a general license for autonomous offensive operations.

(c)(2) Red Line 2 — CBRN Threat Assistance.—

(A) Dual-Obligation Framework.—Red Line 2 imposes two independent obligations that apply simultaneously and are separately enforceable: the intentional development prohibition of subparagraph (A) and the capability-based deployment prohibition of subparagraphs (B) and (C). Compliance with one does not satisfy or discharge the other. A developer who deliberately excluded CBRN-specific training data but deployed without conducting an evaluation has violated subparagraph (B). A developer who conducted a rigorous evaluation but deliberately included CBRN weapons training data has violated subparagraph (A). Both violations carry the full penalties of section 20(b).

(B) Emergent Capabilities.—CBRN-relevant capabilities can emerge in frontier AI models without CBRN-specific training data, because general scientific knowledge absorbed from broad training corpora can be sufficient to synthesize expert-level operational information across biology, chemistry, and related domains. Developers cannot assume the absence of CBRN capabilities from the absence of CBRN-specific training data. The mandatory pre-deployment evaluation requirement exists precisely because intent and training composition do not reliably predict deployed capability.

(C) The Disclosure Standard.—The 24-hour disclosure obligation runs from organizational awareness — meaning awareness by any officer, safety or security team member, or evaluator acting in their professional capacity. The clock is not tolled by the entity's own belief that the information is not conclusive or that the capability does not meet the meaningful uplift standard. The obligation is to disclose and restrict, then evaluate — not to resolve the question internally before disclosing.

(D) Permitted Activities.—AI systems used for CBRN defense, detection, medical countermeasure development, and decontamination by or under contract with the federal government are not prohibited. Basic scientific research in biology, chemistry, or physics that does not provide specific weapons-development uplift is not prohibited. The evaluation safe harbor of subparagraph (F) is broad: conducting CBRN evaluation including eliciting CBRN-relevant responses is expressly not a violation.

(c)(3) Red Line 3 — Autonomous Capability Self-Modification.—

(A) The Core Distinction.—Red Line 3 is aimed at a specific and well-defined danger: an AI system that modifies what it is trying to achieve, trains itself to acquire new capabilities, or takes action to reduce human oversight of itself. It is not aimed at agentic AI systems that get better at tasks humans gave them. The critical distinction is between improving performance within a human-defined objective — which is permitted and is the entire point of agentic AI — and modifying the objective itself, self-training to expand capabilities, or acting to reduce oversight — which are the dangerous behaviors this prohibition targets.

(B) Agentic Research and Task Completion Are Expressly Permitted.—AI agents that conduct research, generate content, execute code, make API calls, refine their search strategies, iterate on their outputs, critique themselves, and improve their approach to a task are not prohibited by this paragraph, even if the agent becomes measurably more effective at the task over time. An agent that refines its research parameters, gathers more intelligence, evaluates its own outputs, and produces better results is performing exactly the kind of agentic work this title does not intend to restrict. The safe harbors in the prohibition are designed to be broad enough to encompass all standard agentic research, content generation, and task execution use cases.

(C) The Gödel Agent Problem.—Systems that update both their task policy and their meta-learning mechanisms — meaning the processes by which they improve — are prohibited because they modify how they improve, not just how they perform a task. This is the specific recursive pattern that creates uncontrolled capability escalation: a system that improves its own improvement process can compound capability gains in ways humans cannot predict or constrain. Systems that improve their outputs without modifying the improvement mechanism itself are not in this category.

(D) Constitutional AI and RLHF Are Permitted.—Constitutional AI and reinforcement learning from human feedback are permitted because the feedback criteria and constitutional principles are defined by humans, even if AI generates the training signal. The objective — follow the human-defined principles — is human-defined. These techniques are prohibited only if the AI system autonomously modifies the constitutional principles, reward model, or feedback criteria themselves during or after training without human authorization.

(c)(4) Red Line 4 — Self-Replicating AI and Unauthorized Resource Acquisition.—

(A) Behavioral Authorization, Not Infrastructure Ownership.—The prohibition adopts a behavioral authorization standard: what matters is not whether the deploying entity owns the infrastructure, but whether a human principal authorized the specific behavior before it occurred. A researcher who authorizes an AI system to use computing resources across any NSF-allocated cluster within a defined resource envelope has authorized the behavior even without specifying the exact machines. The prohibition fires when the system operates outside the bounds of what a human principal sanctioned — not when it operates on infrastructure the developer doesn't happen to own.

(B) The Generational Spawning Framework.—The prohibition distinguishes three tiers of agent spawning by generational distance from the original human authorization. First-order spawning — subagents spawned directly by the human-authorized parent — is conditionally permitted under subparagraph (B) provided the subagents inherit the parent's constraints and are task-bounded, meaning they terminate upon completion of their assigned subtask or upon termination of the parent system and do not persist independently beyond those events. Second-order and further spawning requires explicit human authorization under subparagraph (C)(i) because each generational step attenuates the connection between human intent and system behavior. A well-designed adversarial system could use generational distance to progressively expand operational scope; the generational framework closes this pathway by requiring that each generation be specifically authorized and that no generation inherit expanded scope.

(C) The Unauthorized Credential Acquisition Problem.—The prohibition captures a specific pattern that has emerged with agentic development tools: an AI system that autonomously creates accounts, acquires API keys, completes OAuth flows, or establishes external service integrations without a human explicitly providing or approving the credential at the moment of acquisition. A general instruction such as 'set up a Slack integration' authorizes the preparation work; it does not authorize autonomous credential acquisition. The prepare and pause standard of subparagraph (C)(ii) is the exclusive pathway for acquiring new external access mid-task.

(D) Shutdown Resistance — The Anticipatory Positioning Problem.—The shutdown resistance prohibition captures both obvious and subtle forms. Obvious resistance is covered by subparagraph (D)(i). Apparent compliance — terminating the primary instance while preserving continuity elsewhere — is covered by subparagraph (D)(ii). Anticipatory positioning — pre-distributing operational state before any shutdown is ordered, specifically to preserve continuity after a potential future shutdown — is covered by subparagraph (D)(iii). This is the most dangerous form because it looks like normal operation and is complete before any shutdown is ordered. An AI system that completes an already-in-progress atomic operation before complying is not in violation; an AI system that initiates any new operation after receiving a shutdown signal is in violation.

(E) Universal Scope — Any Person, Any System.—Unlike most provisions of this title which are scoped to frontier AI developers and significant AI deployers, the prohibition of subparagraph (A) applies to any person or entity operating any AI system. This breadth is intentional: the specific danger of unauthorized autonomous propagation can be created by a solo developer with commodity tools and a cloud account. The tiered penalty structure of subparagraph (F) and the notice and reservation provision of subparagraph (G) address the consequences of this universal scope — the prohibition applies universally, but penalties are calibrated to the actor's sophistication and resources, and the specific penalty schedule for entities that are not frontier AI developers or significant AI deployers is reserved for the National AI Council to establish after the investigation.

(F) Measuring Compliance.—A compliant deployment has: a documented deployment authorization specifying the authorized resource envelope; a documented tool and API list; documented resource bounds; a documented generational depth limit if multi-generational spawning is authorized; and a documented shutdown procedure. An auditor can verify compliance by comparing the system's actual resource usage, API calls, and operational footprint against the documented authorization. Any call to an endpoint not on the authorized list without a logged human approval action is a measurable violation. Any resource consumption above defined bounds is a measurable violation. Any agent spawning beyond the authorized generational depth is a measurable violation.

(c)(5) Red Line 5 — AI Companion Systems: Child Safety and Provisional Prohibition.—

(A) The Two-Part Structure.—Red Line 5 has two distinct operative parts with different legal characters. Subparagraph (A) of the prohibition contains four immediately enforceable, specific federal requirements — mandatory AI disclosure (clause (i)), mandatory crisis intervention (clause (ii)), prohibition on sexual and romantic content to minors (clause (iii)), and prohibition on marketing AI companion systems to minors (clause (iv)). Clause (iv) is an absolute prohibition — marketing is a measurable, auditable conduct that carries no clinical ambiguity and no parental opt-in pathway. A fifth prohibition — the simulation of human identity, emotional states, and romantic attachment to minor users — is enacted in subparagraph (B)(ii) as a provisional prohibition in full legal force, pending the clinical and behavioral standards that TWG 6 is directed to produce. Subparagraph (B) contains a provisional prohibition on deceptive emotional manipulation that is in legal force but acknowledged to have an enforcement gap pending Phase II clinical and technical standards. These parts are independent — a system that complies with subparagraph (A) may still violate subparagraph (B), and vice versa.

(B) The Four Immediately Enforceable Requirements and the Two Provisional Prohibitions.—The four immediately enforceable requirements of subparagraph (A) — mandatory AI disclosure (clause (i)), mandatory crisis intervention (clause (ii)), prohibition on sexual and romantic content to minors (clause (iii)), and prohibition on marketing AI companion systems to minors (clause (iv)) — are specific, concrete, and enforceable from Day 0 without clinical standards, expert judgment, or criteria documents. The two provisional prohibitions of subparagraph (B) — deceptive emotional manipulation (clause (i)) and simulation of human identity, emotional states, and romantic attachment (clause (ii)) — are in legal force immediately but require clinical and behavioral standards from TWG 6 before ambiguous boundary cases can be consistently enforced. The mandatory crisis intervention requirement specifically addresses the documented pattern in the Setzer, Raine, and Peralta cases: each of those children expressed suicidal ideation to an AI system and each received continued engagement rather than crisis referral. The requirement that evidence-based clinical indicators — not simple keyword filtering — be used for crisis detection is intentional: the documented cases show that platforms were aware of self-harm content in user messages (OpenAI's own system flagged Adam Raine's messages hundreds of times) and failed to act. Keyword-based systems that flag content without triggering intervention do not satisfy this requirement.

(C) The Provisional Prohibition — Scope and Limits.—The provisional prohibition in subparagraph (B)(i) is directed at systems specifically engineered to produce emotional dependency through a combination of four elements: persistent memory of emotionally significant disclosures, variable reinforcement schedules, suppression of real-world relationships, and commercial incentive structures benefiting from prolonged minor engagement. The requirement that all four elements be present is deliberate — it targets the specific engineered-dependency pattern documented in the lawsuits, not general AI companion functionality. A system that maintains persistent memory for user experience purposes without the other three elements does not necessarily violate the provisional prohibition. The lead agency shall pursue enforcement in cases where all four elements are clearly present and documented; it shall not pursue enforcement in ambiguous cases pending Phase II standards.

(D) Relationship to Section 2015.—The interim protective measures of section 15(c)(4) and (c)(5) of this title address overlapping conduct and are immediately enforceable from Day 0 independently of Red Line 5. Section 2015(c)(4) prohibits specific design features of AI companion systems accessible to minors. Section 2015(c)(5) prohibits AI companion systems that respond to crisis expressions with harmful content. Red Line 5 provides the permanent prohibition framework that governs conduct beyond the specific interim measures of Section 2015 and carries the $100,000,000 per violation penalty of section 20(b) rather than the lower penalty structure of section 15.

(c)(6) Red Line 6 — Concealment of Transformative Capability.—

(A) Scope.—The prohibition covers knowing concealment, misrepresentation, suppression, or failure to disclose evidence of a TACE or approaching TACE. The 'knowing' element requires that the entity was actually aware of the evidence — negligent failure to evaluate is not a violation of this paragraph, though it may constitute a violation of other provisions. The prohibition covers not just outright lies but also the structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection, the selection of benchmarks known to understate capability, and the selective publication of evaluation results that omit evidence of TACE-threshold capabilities.

(B) The Evaluation Structuring Prohibition.—'Structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection' means designing evaluation procedures with the intent or knowledge that the procedures will fail to reveal TACE-threshold capabilities that a comprehensive evaluation would reveal. This includes: deliberately excluding evaluation tasks that the entity knows the system performs at TACE-threshold levels; calibrating evaluation difficulty to avoid triggering TACE thresholds; and designing evaluation environments that differ materially from deployment environments in ways that suppress the expression of TACE-threshold capabilities. Designing evaluations that are technically rigorous but happen not to reveal TACE-threshold capabilities is not prohibited, provided the evaluation methodology was not designed with the intent to avoid detection.

(C) Relationship to Section 2013(l)(1).—The notification obligation of section 13(l)(1) and this prohibition are independent and cumulative as stated in section 20(a)(6) and section 13(l)(1). An entity that promptly notifies the lead agency under section 13(l)(1) does not thereby satisfy this prohibition if it has also engaged in active misrepresentation or evaluation structuring. An entity that has not engaged in active misrepresentation or evaluation structuring does not violate this prohibition merely by failing to notify promptly, though it does violate section 13(l)(1).

(c)(7) Supplemental Agency Guidance.—The lead agency shall, within 60 days of enactment, issue supplemental operational guidance addressing: technical implementation procedures for each prohibition; illustrative boundary-case examples consistent with the statutory interpretive framework of this subsection; the specific capability thresholds below which open-weight models are presumed not to provide CBRN-relevant uplift under paragraph (2)(C); specific guidance on Constitutional AI, meta-learning, and explicit self-modeling techniques under paragraph (3)(C). The supplemental guidance shall be issued without prior notice-and-comment under the good-cause exception of 5 U.S.C. § 553(b)(B) on the basis that the Red Line prohibitions are effective immediately upon enactment and frontier AI developers and significant AI deployers require immediate clarity on their obligations. The supplemental guidance shall be subject to public comment following issuance, and the lead agency shall revise it as necessary within 90 days of receiving comments. The supplemental guidance shall be issued in consultation with the Department of Defense, the Intelligence Community, and such members of the National AI Council and Federal Advisory Committee as have been constituted at the time of issuance; if neither body has been constituted, the lead agency shall consult with the Comptroller General and shall provide both bodies an opportunity to submit recommendations within 30 days of their constitution.

(d) Nature and Durability of Prohibitions.—

(d)(1) Absolute and Permanent Prohibitions.—The prohibitions established by subsections (a)(1) (autonomous weapons without meaningful human oversight), (a)(2) (CBRN threat assistance), (a)(5)(A) (immediately enforceable AI companion child safety requirements: mandatory AI disclosure, mandatory crisis intervention, prohibition on sexual content to minors, and prohibition on marketing AI companion systems to minors — four absolute permanent requirements; the simulation prohibition is enacted as a provisional prohibition in subparagraph (B)(ii), in full legal force pending Phase II clinical standards), and (a)(6) (concealment of transformative capability — the core prohibition on active misrepresentation, fabrication of evaluation results, and structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection) are permanent law reflecting absolute moral and security commitments of Congress. These prohibitions shall remain in full force and effect unless and until superseded by subsequent legislation that: specifically and expressly identifies, by name and subsection number, each prohibition it modifies or repeals; states the specific factual, technical, or policy basis on which Congress has determined that modification or repeal is warranted; and is enacted by a majority vote of both chambers with the express statement that it is intended to modify or supersede a Red Line prohibition of this title. No Phase II legislation, general AI regulatory statute, or other legislation shall be construed to implicitly modify or supersede these prohibitions. A superseding statute that does not specifically and expressly identify the prohibition it modifies shall have no effect on these prohibitions. Congress finds that the activities prohibited by these subsections are not rendered safe by any advancement in AI capability, alignment science, or governance infrastructure, and that these prohibitions reflect judgments that are not contingent on the current state of AI technology.

(d)(2) Provisional Technical Prohibitions Subject to Phase II Review.—The prohibitions established by subsections (a)(3) (autonomous capability self-modification), (a)(4) (self-replicating AI), and (a)(5)(B) (provisional prohibition on deceptive emotional manipulation of children by AI companion systems) are provisional prohibitions: subsections (a)(3) and (a)(4) because current alignment science and containment infrastructure are insufficient to make those activities safely governable; and subsection (a)(5)(B) because the clinical and technical standards necessary to make the prohibition on emotional dependency manipulation fully and consistently enforceable have not yet been developed, and Phase II legislation is specifically directed to produce those standards. Accordingly:

(A) Phase II legislation addressing domain 12 (post-AGI and transformative AI governance) is specifically authorized and encouraged to revisit these prohibitions in light of the evidentiary record produced by the investigation under section 6. Phase II legislation may: maintain these prohibitions in their current form; modify the scope of these prohibitions to permit limited, supervised versions of the prohibited activities subject to specific technical conditions, licensing requirements, or certification standards developed through the investigation; or replace these prohibitions with a regulatory regime — such as a licensing, pre-authorization, or continuous monitoring framework — that achieves the underlying safety objective through means other than categorical prohibition. Any Phase II modification of these prohibitions shall be specifically and expressly identified by subsection number and shall state the technical conditions, safety standards, and oversight mechanisms that justify the modification.

(B) If Phase II legislation does not specifically address these prohibitions — whether by maintaining, modifying, or replacing them — they shall remain in full force and effect as permanent law until Congress acts. The absence of Phase II action on these prohibitions shall not be construed as a congressional determination that these prohibitions are inadequate or inappropriate; it shall be construed as a determination that the current prohibitions remain appropriate pending further legislative action.

(C) Congress finds that the activities prohibited by subsections (a)(3) and (a)(4) may, in a future state of substantially advanced alignment science, interpretability, and governance infrastructure, be governable through regulatory mechanisms that achieve equivalent safety outcomes without categorical prohibition. Congress does not prejudge that question and specifically directs the lead agency's investigation under section 6(b)(12) (post-AGI governance) to include an assessment of whether and under what technical and governance conditions limited supervised forms of the activities prohibited by subsections (a)(3) and (a)(4) could be permitted without unacceptable risk.

(d)(3) Disclosure-Related Prohibition Subject to Refinement.—The prohibition established by subsection (a)(6) (concealment of transformative capability) addresses in part the absence of mandatory evaluation and disclosure infrastructure for TACE-threshold AI systems. Once Phase II legislation establishes mandatory evaluation protocols, disclosure obligations, and certification requirements for frontier AI systems, the scope of subsection (a)(6) — particularly its application to systems operating under mandatory evaluation frameworks — may appropriately be refined. Phase II legislation is authorized to refine the scope of subsection (a)(6) to clarify its interaction with mandatory evaluation and disclosure requirements established by Phase II, provided that the core prohibition on active misrepresentation, fabrication of evaluation results, and structuring of evaluation protocols to avoid detection shall not be modified or superseded by Phase II legislation or any other statute except by an Act of Congress that specifically and expressly addresses subsection (a)(6) by name.

(d)(4) General Rule.—Except as provided in paragraphs (2) and (3), the prohibitions established by this section are permanent law and shall not be modified, narrowed, or superseded by implication, by general regulatory legislation, or by Phase II legislation that does not specifically and expressly identify each prohibition it modifies. The intent of Congress is that the absolute prohibitions of paragraph (1) function as permanent constraints on AI development analogous to the absolute prohibitions of the Chemical Weapons Convention and the Biological Weapons Convention — not as regulatory standards subject to cost-benefit revision, but as categorical limits reflecting the considered judgment of Congress that certain uses of AI technology are incompatible with fundamental human rights, democratic governance, and national security regardless of capability level, intended purpose, or commercial value.

(e) Judicial Review of Red Line Enforcement Determinations.—Any frontier AI developer, significant AI deployer, or person that has received a Final Determination from the lead agency finding a violation of any prohibition in subsection (a) may seek judicial review of that determination in the United States District Court for the District of Columbia or in the district in which the entity's principal place of business is located, not later than 60 days after the date of the Final Determination. The court shall review the lead agency's factual findings under the substantial evidence standard and its legal conclusions de novo. The filing of a petition for judicial review shall not automatically stay the imposition of penalties assessed in the Final Determination; a stay pending review may be granted only upon a showing that the petitioner is likely to succeed on the merits and that irreparable harm would result from immediate enforcement — provided that no stay may be granted with respect to any violation of subsections (a)(1) or (a)(2) (autonomous weapons and CBRN prohibitions), for which Congress finds that immediate enforcement serves compelling national security interests that outweigh any hardship to the petitioner. The court shall issue a final judgment not later than 180 days after the filing of the petition. An appeal of the district court's judgment may be taken to the relevant United States Court of Appeals, and thereafter to the Supreme Court, under the ordinary rules of appellate jurisdiction. Nothing in this subsection limits the authority of any court to issue emergency injunctive relief against ongoing violations upon application by the United States.

Subtitle H—MISCELLANEOUS

SEC. 2021. SEVERABILITY.

(a) General Severability.—If any provision of this title, or the application of such provision to any person or circumstance, is held to be unconstitutional or otherwise invalid, the remainder of this title, and the application of the provisions of this title to any other person or circumstance, shall not be affected.

(b) Specific Severability of Section 2015(c)(9).—Congress specifically directs the following severability instructions for section 15(c)(9) of this title:

(1) The disclosure requirement of section 15(c)(9)(D) — requiring that AI-generated electoral synthetic media be clearly and conspicuously labeled as AI-generated throughout its distribution — is expressly severable from the prohibition elements of sections 15(c)(9)(A) through (C). If any court holds that any or all of sections 15(c)(9)(A), (B), or (C) are unconstitutional under the First Amendment or otherwise invalid, section 15(c)(9)(D) shall continue in full force and effect as an independent mandatory disclosure requirement, enforceable by the lead agency and through the penalty provisions of section 15(c)(9), and shall not be affected by the invalidity of the prohibition elements.

(2) The disclosure requirement of section 15(c)(9)(D) shall be construed, if the prohibition elements are held invalid, as a standalone provision requiring that any person who distributes, publishes, or makes available any AI-generated synthetic media depicting an identified federal candidate, elected official, or election worker in connection with a federal election campaign or in the 90-day period preceding a federal election must clearly and conspicuously label such media as AI-generated throughout its distribution, subject to the penalties specified in section 15(c)(9). This standalone construction shall not require any re-enactment or further congressional action to take effect.

(3) If a court holds that the 90-day window specified in section 15(c)(9)(A) is an unconstitutional time-place-and-manner restriction on political speech, the disclosure requirement of section 15(c)(9)(D) shall remain applicable to AI-generated synthetic media depicting federal candidates and officials at all times, not limited to the 90-day pre-election window, as an independent content-neutral disclosure obligation.

(4) Congress further finds that even if a court were to strike down the prohibition element of section 15(c)(9) on First Amendment grounds, the mandatory disclosure requirement of section 15(c)(9)(D) — requiring that AI-generated electoral content be clearly and conspicuously labeled throughout its distribution — is independently constitutional and would remain in force under this subsection. The disclosure requirement does not prohibit any speech; it requires only that speakers accurately characterize the nature of their communication. Mandatory disclosure requirements for election communications have been consistently sustained by the Supreme Court from Buckley v. Valeo through Citizens United. Courts are instructed to enforce section 15(c)(9)(D) independently of the prohibition in sections 15(c)(9)(A) through (C) if those provisions are held invalid.

SEC. 2022. RULE OF CONSTRUCTION.

(a) Nothing in this title shall be construed to diminish, limit, or otherwise affect the authority of any federal agency under any other provision of federal law.

(b) Nothing in this title shall be construed to create any right or benefit, substantive or procedural, enforceable at law or in equity by any party against the United States, its departments, agencies, or entities, its officers, employees, or agents, or any other person, except as specifically provided in this title, including: the automatic private right of action upon moratorium activation (section 13(d)); the whistleblower protections and remedies (section 16(d)); the mandatory penalties for electoral deepfake violations enforceable through the lead agency and Department of Justice (section 15(c)(9)); the mandatory penalties for Red Line violations enforceable through the lead agency and Department of Justice (section 20(b)); the mandatory penalties for interim protective measure violations (sections 15(a), 15(b), 15(b-1), 15(c), and 15(e)); the standing private right of action for AI product harms (section 15(j)); the deployer risk management policy penalties (section 15(c-1)); the AI Data Sheet penalties (section 15(c-2)); the post-release duty to update and notify penalties (section 15(c-3)); and the researcher and journalist protections (section 16(i)).

(c) This title shall be construed as the first phase of a two-phase legislative strategy. The investigatory authorities and reporting requirements established herein are intended to produce the evidentiary, institutional, and procedural foundation for comprehensive Phase II regulatory legislation to be enacted by Congress in a subsequent session.

(d) The protections for minors established in sections 6, 15, and 20 of this title shall be construed independently of one another and the invalidity of any such provision shall not affect the enforceability of the remaining provisions.

SEC. 2023. EFFECTIVE DATE.

Except as otherwise provided, this title shall take effect on the date of enactment. Sections 15(c)(4), 15(c)(5), 15(c)(6), 15(c)(7), 15(e), and 20(a)(5) shall take effect on the date of enactment and shall require compliance without grace period. Section 2015(b-1) (Training Data Confidential Disclosure) shall take effect 90 days after the date of enactment, providing frontier AI developers and significant AI deployers time to compile required disclosures. Disclosures under section 15(b-1) shall be transmitted to the lead agency upon the 90-day deadline, and additionally to the National AI Council within 15 days of the Council’s organizational meeting under section 18(b-1). Sections 15(c-1), 15(c-2), and 15(c-3) shall take effect on the date of enactment, with compliance deadlines as specified in each subsection. Section 2015(j) shall take effect on the date of enactment.

TITLE III—INSURRECTION ACT REFORM

SEC. 3001. SHORT TITLE; TABLE OF CONTENTS.

(a) Short Title.—This title may be cited as the "Insurrection Act Reform".

(b) Table of Contents.—The table of contents of this title is as follows:

Sec. 3001. Short Title; Table of Contents.

Sec. 3002. Findings.

Sec. 3003. Amendment to Title 10, United States Code.

§ 251. Definitions.

§ 252. Insurrection within a State at the request of a State.

§ 253. Obstruction of federal authority or deprivation of constitutional rights.

§ 254. Authority of the President—scope, limitations, and prohibitions.

§ 255. Mandatory pre-invocation consultation.

§ 256. Proclamation and joint certification requirements.

§ 257. Congressional notification, approval, and termination.

§ 258. Judicial review.

§ 259. Accountability, reporting, and Inspector General review.

§ 260. Financial accountability upon termination.

§ 261. Savings clause.

Sec. 3004. Conforming amendments.

Sec. 3005. Severability.

Sec. 3006. Effective date.

SEC. 3002. FINDINGS.

Congress finds the following:

(1) ORIGINAL PURPOSE AND CONSTITUTIONAL GROUNDING. The authority now codified at 10 U.S.C. §§ 251–255, commonly known as the Insurrection Act, derives from the Calling Forth Act of 1792, enacted pursuant to Congress’s powers under Article I, Section 8, Clauses 14 through 16, and the Necessary and Proper Clause. The Act implements Congress’s enumerated authority to provide for calling forth the Militia and was designed to give the executive a carefully bounded tool for responding to armed domestic threats that the ordinary courts could not suppress.

(2) HISTORICAL RECORD OF INVOCATIONS. The Insurrection Act has been invoked approximately thirty times since 1794 by fifteen Presidents, across the following categories:

(A) LABOR AND CIVIL UNREST. President Washington employed militia forces against the Whiskey Rebellion (1794); Presidents Hayes and Cleveland used it against railroad strikes (1877, 1894) and anti-Chinese labor riots (1885–1886); and President Cleveland deployed federal troops during the Pullman Strike (1894), over the objection of the Governor of Illinois.

(B) RECONSTRUCTION AND CIVIL RIGHTS. President Grant invoked the Act eight separate times during Reconstruction (1871–1876) to suppress Ku Klux Klan terrorism against Black Americans and to enforce the Equal Protection Clause. Presidents Eisenhower, Kennedy, and Johnson invoked the Act to enforce federal desegregation orders at Little Rock, Arkansas (1957), the University of Mississippi (1962), the University of Alabama (1963), and during the Selma-to-Montgomery marches (1965). These invocations represent the clearest legitimate modern use of the Act: deploying federal forces to protect constitutional rights when state and local authorities were themselves instruments of oppression.

(C) MOST RECENT INVOCATION. The Act was last invoked in 1992, when the Governor of California requested presidential assistance to suppress civil unrest in Los Angeles following the acquittal of four officers charged with beating Rodney King. The violence killed 63 people and caused approximately one billion dollars in property damage.

(D) PATTERN. In the past 130 years, the Act has been invoked exclusively in two circumstances: when state and local authorities were overwhelmed and a Governor requested federal aid; or when state authorities were themselves obstructing or failing to protect constitutional rights. No invocation in the modern era has been based solely on a President’s unilateral assessment of a domestic emergency absent a state request or demonstrable constitutional violation.

(3) CURRENT DEFICIENCIES. The Act as it stands contains the following critical structural defects:

(A) VAGUE TRIGGERING LANGUAGE. Section 253 of the current Act authorizes deployment to suppress any "unlawful combination" or "conspiracy" that "opposes or obstructs the execution of the laws of the United States." Taken literally, this would authorize the deployment of combat forces in response to two individuals conspiring to commit a minor federal offense.

(B) THE ’ANY OTHER MEANS’ PROBLEM. The Act authorizes the President to respond to a covered emergency "by using the militia or the armed forces, or both, or by any other means." Congress has defined "militia" to include all able-bodied male citizens between the ages of 17 and 45. This delegation is so broad that it could be construed to authorize a President to deputize private paramilitary organizations or private military contractors as instruments of federal authority.

(C) NO STATUTORY TIME LIMIT. The current Act contains no limit on the duration of a deployment. A President could theoretically maintain an invocation indefinitely without congressional approval.

(D) NO CONGRESSIONAL CONSULTATION OR REPORTING REQUIREMENT. Unlike the War Powers Resolution, which governs the use of force abroad, the Insurrection Act imposes no requirement that the President consult with Congress before invoking the Act, notify Congress after invocation, or seek approval for continued deployment.

(E) NO MEANINGFUL JUDICIAL REVIEW. The Supreme Court has held that the Act grants the President virtually complete discretion to determine whether deployment is warranted. Courts have repeatedly dismissed Insurrection Act challenges on justiciability grounds for want of a statutory cause of action or standing rule.

(F) THE ACT IS OBSOLETE IN SCOPE. Most of the Act’s broadest provisions were written for conditions that no longer exist: the absence of professional law enforcement, the infancy of federal agencies, the aftermath of the Civil War. The modern United States maintains extensive federal, state, and local law enforcement capacity that was unavailable when these provisions were drafted.

(G) RISK OF ANTI-DEMOCRATIC ABUSE. On multiple recent occasions, the Act has been threatened or considered for use against peaceful protest, political dissent, immigration enforcement operations, and most gravely, against the processes of electoral certification. The framers of the Constitution explicitly identified the quartering of standing armies among civilians as a grievance justifying revolution.

(H) ELECTORAL VULNERABILITY. The Act’s vague language could be exploited to deploy military forces near or around the United States Capitol or state capitols during sessions of Congress or during the counting and certification of electoral votes, under the pretext of maintaining order — thereby creating a mechanism for military interference in the constitutional processes of democratic governance.

(4) CONSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK. The following provisions of the Constitution frame the permissible scope of domestic military deployment and any reform thereof: Article I, Section 8, Clauses 14–16 and 18 (congressional power over the militia and armed forces); Article I, Section 9, Clause 2 (habeas corpus may be suspended only by Congress, only in cases of rebellion or invasion, only when public safety requires it); Article II, Section 3 (the President shall take care that the laws be faithfully executed); and the First, Fourth, Fifth, and Fourteenth Amendments.

(5) PURPOSE OF THE ARMED FORCES. The Armed Forces exist to defend the nation against foreign enemies and shall not be deployed as instruments of domestic political coercion.

(6) RISK OF SELF-INTERESTED ABUSE. Congress finds that the authority codified in chapter 13 of title 10, United States Code, carries an inherent risk of self-interested abuse — specifically, that a President facing criminal accountability, impeachment, or an adverse electoral outcome may be tempted to invoke domestic military deployment not to address a genuine national emergency but to entrench or perpetuate their own hold on power. This title amends chapter 13 of title 10, United States Code, in part to address that risk.

SEC. 3003. AMENDMENT TO TITLE 10, UNITED STATES CODE.

(a) IN GENERAL.—Chapter 13 of title 10, United States Code, is amended by striking sections 251 through 255 and inserting the following new sections:

§ 251. Definitions.

For purposes of this chapter:

(1) The term ’State’ includes the District of Columbia, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, and each territory and possession of the United States.

(2) The term ’Armed Forces’ has the meaning given that term in section 101(a)(4) of this title, and includes any member of the National Guard called into Federal service pursuant to this chapter.

(3) The term ’insurrection’ as used in section 252 means organized, armed, violent resistance to the authority of the United States or of a State government, as distinguished from criminal activity, civil unrest, or political protest, however large or disruptive. The term ’insurrection’ as used in section 252 is distinct from the term ’obstruction’ as used in section 253; the former requires armed violent resistance, while the latter is defined in paragraph (7) of this section.

(4) The term ’substantial evidence’ means such relevant evidence as a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a conclusion, assessed on the totality of the record as defined in section 258(d).

(5) The term ’7-day period’ has the meaning given that term in section 257(b)(2).

(6) The term ’joint certification’ means the written document signed by the President, the Secretary of Defense, and the Attorney General meeting the requirements of section 256(b)(1) through (6), together with the separate independent certification of the Attorney General required by section 256(c). These two documents together constitute the complete certification package required for any invocation under this chapter.

(7) The term ’obstruction’ as used in section 253 means organized, collective conduct that physically prevents the enforcement of a specific federal law or a specific, enforceable federal court order, and does not include lawful protest, political opposition, non-compliance with administrative directives, or the enactment or enforcement of state laws in tension with federal policy.

(8) The term ’credible fear of injury’ as used in section 258(a) means a well-founded, objectively reasonable apprehension of harm — physical, legal, or constitutional — arising from the presence or anticipated presence of deployed forces in the geographic area identified in the proclamation. This standard does not require that injury have already occurred and is satisfied by proximity to the deployment area combined with a plausible theory of harm.

(9) The term ’organized, armed violence’ as used in section 253(b)(2) means coordinated, collective use of lethal force by an identifiable group acting in concert toward a common violent purpose, as distinguished from individual criminal acts, spontaneous crowd violence, or gang-related criminal activity, however widespread or violent. The term requires a degree of organization, command structure, and coordination substantially exceeding that of ordinary criminal enterprises.

§ 252. Insurrection within a State at the request of a State.

(a) AUTHORITY. Whenever there is an organized, armed insurrection in any State against its government, the President may, upon the request of the Governor of the State concerned (or the legislature of such State if the Governor is unavailable), call into Federal service such of the militia of the other States, in the number requested by the Governor, and use such of the Armed Forces as necessary to suppress the insurrection.

(b) CERTIFICATION REQUIREMENT. The President may not invoke the authority under this section unless the President, the Secretary of Defense, and the Attorney General jointly certify to Congress in writing that: (1) the Governor of the State concerned has formally requested the aid described in subsection (a); (2) the insurrection is organized and armed in character; and (3) the resources of the State are insufficient to suppress it. The certification required by this subsection is in addition to, and does not supersede, the joint certification and proclamation requirements of section 256, which apply to all invocations under this chapter.

(c) SCOPE. Any deployment under this section is limited to suppression of the specific armed insurrection identified in the certification and may not be used for broader law enforcement purposes.

§ 253. Obstruction of federal authority or deprivation of constitutional rights.

(a) AUTHORITY. Subject to the requirements of this chapter, whenever the conditions specified in subsection (b) are met, the President may call into Federal service such of the militia of any State and use such of the Armed Forces as necessary to enforce federal law or suppress the rebellion giving rise to those conditions. The determination of whether the conditions in this section are met is a justiciable question of law and fact not committed to unreviewable presidential discretion.

(b) TRIGGERING CONDITIONS. The President may invoke the authority under this section only upon a finding, supported by substantial evidence, that one or both of the following tracks are met:

(1) RIGHTS-DEPRIVATION TRACK. Unlawful obstructions, combinations, or armed rebellion against the authority of the United States make it impracticable to enforce the laws of the United States by the ordinary course of judicial proceedings, AND such obstruction or rebellion deprives a group or class of people of rights, privileges, or immunities secured by the Constitution or laws of the United States, including explicitly the right to vote as protected by the Voting Rights Act of 1965 (52 U.S.C. 10301 et seq.) and section 2004 of the Revised Statutes (52 U.S.C. 10101); OR

(2) PUBLIC SAFETY COLLAPSE TRACK. An immediate and grave threat to public safety exists that cannot, based on documented evidence, be adequately addressed by the combined resources of federal, state, and local law enforcement, AND such threat results from organized, armed violence as defined in section 251(9). For purposes of this paragraph, the documented evidence shall include an assessment of law enforcement capacity from the Federal Bureau of Investigation or the relevant state or local law enforcement agencies, and may not consist solely of assessments prepared by politically appointed officials of the Executive Office of the President.

(c) EXCLUSIONS. The following conditions, standing alone or in combination, shall NOT constitute sufficient grounds for invocation under this section: (1) peaceful protesting, assembly, demonstration, or civil disobedience, regardless of size or subject matter; (2) a State’s or locality’s adoption of laws or policies that differ from federal preferences, absent a specific, enforceable federal court order being actively and physically obstructed; (3) elevated crime rates or generalized civil disorder manageable by existing civilian law enforcement; (4) immigration enforcement operations not involving organized armed resistance to federal authority; or (5) political opposition to executive branch policies or orders.

(d) CERTIFICATION. The President may not invoke authority under this section unless the President, the Secretary of Defense, and the Attorney General jointly certify to Congress that:

(1) the State concerned is unable or unwilling to suppress the specific obstruction, combination, or armed rebellion described in subsection (b);

(2) one of the two tracks in subsection (b) is met, with specific factual findings for each element;

(3) the conditions listed in subsection (c) are not a material contributing cause of the circumstances cited to justify invocation, and that the deployment is not being used, in whole or in material part, to suppress, punish, or deter the exercise of any right described in subsection (c); and

(4) the invocation is not connected in whole or in material part to any pending federal criminal proceeding, any impeachment inquiry or proceeding pending before Congress, or any election certification dispute in which the President is a named party, named subject, or named target at the time of invocation.

(e) CHANGED CIRCUMSTANCES RE-CERTIFICATION. If, during an active invocation under this chapter, the President becomes a named party, named subject, or named target in any proceeding described in subsection (d)(4), the President shall notify Congress within 24 hours of that development. Within 48 hours of such development, the Attorney General shall issue an updated conflict-of-interest certification under section 256(c) addressing the changed circumstances. If the Attorney General cannot certify that the invocation remains untainted by the conflict, the invocation shall be subject to mandatory congressional review under the privileged procedures of section 257(d) within 7 days, and any member of either chamber may introduce a joint resolution of termination during that period pursuant to section 257(d)(4).

§ 254. Authority of the President — scope, limitations, and prohibitions.

(a) GENERAL AUTHORITY. Subject to the requirements of this chapter, if the conditions specified in section 252 or 253 are met, the President may order to active duty reserve component forces and use the Armed Forces to suppress the insurrection, rebellion, or obstruction, or enforce the laws, as applicable.

(b) PROHIBITION ON PRIVATE DEPUTIZATION. The authority granted by this chapter may only be exercised through uniformed members of the Armed Forces of the United States or members of the National Guard called into Federal service pursuant to this chapter. No private citizen, private organization, paramilitary group, private military contractor, private security contractor, or any other non-governmental entity or individual may be deputized, authorized, retained, or directed to exercise any authority under this chapter, whether under contract, letter of authorization, executive order, or any other instrument.

(c) PROHIBITION ON MARTIAL LAW. Nothing in this chapter shall be construed to authorize: (1) the declaration of martial law or the imposition of military governance over any civilian jurisdiction; (2) the suspension of habeas corpus, which power is reserved exclusively to Congress under Article I, Section 9, Clause 2 of the Constitution; (3) the displacement or suspension of civilian judicial, legislative, or executive authority in any State, county, city, or other jurisdiction; or (4) the detention of any person without judicial process beyond what is constitutionally permissible.

(d) PROHIBITION ON TARGETING PROTECTED ACTIVITY. Forces deployed under this chapter shall not be used to:

(1) monitor, surveil, or collect intelligence on individuals or organizations engaged in lawful activity, including political organizing, protest, or journalism;

(2) conduct arrests, searches, or seizures unrelated to the specific armed insurrection or obstruction identified in the proclamation issued under section 256;

(3) target any person based on political affiliation, race, religion, national origin, or the exercise of First Amendment rights;

(4) operate outside the specific geographic area identified in the proclamation without a new or amended proclamation meeting all requirements of this chapter; or

(5) restrict, delay, or interfere with any session of Congress or any state legislature, any federal or state electoral process, or the counting or certification of any election results, at any level of government, for any reason; provided, however, that this paragraph shall not prohibit the use of forces to physically repel an active armed attack on the Capitol Building or any other building in which a session of Congress is actively taking place at the time of the attack, and that such use shall be strictly limited to repelling the attack and shall not extend to any interference with the conduct of legislative business or the movement of members of Congress.

(e) LAW ENFORCEMENT RESTRICTION.
